So... actually you are catching client side (requests.session to be precise) connection timeout, not server side one.
Then ok, but please, at least fix travis. 

For the object thing, this might work (didn't test it, don't have the time for it this evening)
```python
try:
    res = self._request(req_and_resp, **kwargs)
except (RequestsConnectionError, Timeout) as e:
    req, res = req_and_resp  # this is a tuple of pyswagger.io.Request, and Response, so you can use it :)
    res.status = 500
    res._Response__path = req._Request__path  # request__path is the same as response, and is always set
    
    try:
        res.data = e.message.message
    except AttributeError:
        res.data = ""
```

active1表示有成交变动的一次快照。行情虽然是每3秒推送一次，但实际接收到有时候会丢，也就是间隔6秒。
active1增加1，表示这期间发生过成交。如果没有成交，每次快照给出的时间戳、五档价格和委托量都是可能变动的，但active1不变。如果active1增加1，那么总量，总金额一定会变。因此active1体现的是个股是否成交活跃。
reversed_bytes0是时间，是一个int，从左往右依次为HHMMSSss，例如9454674表示9:45:46.74，13122010表示13:12:20.10，最后2位数字是否是毫秒不是很确定，可以忽略。
reversed_bytes1 是一个负整数，去掉负号等于当前价格*100。

What image dtypes and shapes are you using?
The following code works for me:

```python
import imgaug as ia
from imgaug import augmenters as iaa
from scipy import misc
import random
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from imgaug import parameters as iap
from skimage import data

images = np.uint8([data.astronaut()])
poses = np.int32([
    [20, 50, 70, 110]
])

images = np.tile(images, (16, 1, 1, 1))
poses = np.tile(poses, (16, 1))

### input images, poses ###
keypoints_on_images = []
for img_idx, (image, pose) in enumerate(zip(images, poses)):
    height, width = image.shape[0:2]
    keypoints = []
    for i in range(int(poses.shape[1] / 2)):
        x = np.clip(int(poses[img_idx][i * 2]), 0, 255)
        y = np.clip(int(poses[img_idx][i * 2 + 1]), 0, 255)
        keypoints.append(ia.Keypoint(x=x, y=y))
    keypoints_on_images.append(ia.KeypointsOnImage(keypoints, shape=image.shape))


seq = iaa.Sequential([iaa.CropAndPad(percent=(-0.25, 0.25))])
seq_det = seq.to_deterministic() # call this for each batch again, NOT only once at the start

images_aug = seq_det.augment_images(images)
keypoints_aug = seq_det.augment_keypoints(keypoints_on_images)

print(images_aug.shape)

# Example code to show each image and print the new keypoints coordinates
for img_idx, (image_before, image_after, keypoints_before, keypoints_after) in enumerate(zip(images, images_aug, keypoints_on_images, keypoints_aug)):
    image_before = keypoints_before.draw_on_image(image_before)
    image_after = keypoints_after.draw_on_image(image_after)
    plt.imshow(np.concatenate((image_before, image_after), axis=1)) # before and after
    print(image_before.shape, np.min(image_before), np.average(image_before), np.max(image_before))
    print(image_after.shape, np.min(image_after), np.average(image_after), np.max(image_after))
    plt.show()
    misc.imsave("tmp.jpg", np.concatenate((image_before, image_after), axis=1))
    for kp_idx, keypoint in enumerate(keypoints_after.keypoints):
        keypoint_old = keypoints_on_images[img_idx].keypoints[kp_idx]
        x_old, y_old = keypoint_old.x, keypoint_old.y
        x_new, y_new = keypoint.x, keypoint.y
        print("[Keypoints for image #%d] before aug: x=%d y=%d | after aug: x=%d y=%d" % (img_idx, x_old, y_old, x_new, y_new))
```

Programmed functions would still fall in one the above categories; implicit or explicit. After making this distinction they should still be treated in the same way as their symbolic counterparts.

And don't get me wrong, a model is a model is a model. The whole point of symfit is that for every one of these models you can just evaluate it at the points you desire, without having to care whether some integration has to be done in the background. So in that sense nothing is distinguished or treated differently. But of course internally they are different, so this has to be accounted for.

As an extra note, programmed functions are something I want to actively discourage. I see it as a slippery slope to writing the same unreadable mess you see in other libraries. However over the summer I encountered a model which was not writable as a symbolic function so I ended up using ROOT to solve it. For such cases it might be nice have this capability, but I'm already afraid people are going to use it for everything if the capability exists, which is very undesirable.

Interactivity is a pretty tricky question with `runipy` as it is a batch processing tool.

Some interactive elements that require immediate user feedback (i.e. blocking elements) will never be supported directly. There are some interactive elements that are non-blocking. Thanks for replying! I was trying to make the tests more understandable and approachable. A few notes:

* The main problem was that the tests weren't runnable in a virtual environment (`pyenv` or `virtualenv`) which is how the vast majority of python developers work nowadays. Removing the shelling out from the tests was both a fix for this, and a move towards a more proper split between testing the _functionality_ of the code, and the I/O, or method of running the code.
* The examples still work if ran as non-executables (e.g. `python -m examples.booklet`). I prefer to run these this way because it avoids confusion with python paths (see [this](https://github.com/asottile/scratch/wiki/PythonPathSadness)), and it doesn't rely on system-installed python (see the above point on virtualenvs). You can keep them as executable files with hash bangs, but that's not really any better than as python modules (that, furthermore, have their code not at the global level so they're actually importable and unit-testable).
* Packaging the PDFs as a python package is also towards the goal of running the tests with one setup (`pip install -r requirements-dev.txt`) and one run (`nosetests tests` or whatever) command, from the top-level directory of your repo. If they're in a python package, all your dependencies for running your tests can be specified in one place. Before, I had to run the tests and see failures/read tracebacks to figure out what the missing dependencies were. Just put all your test dependencies in `requirements-dev.txt`. Install that into your virtualenv and you're ready to go.

If you have no interest in making the tests more user-friendly, that's totally fine. Everyone's got their own style :). I was just trying to get them running and it took me quite a while. Maybe a better change would be putting more explicit instructions front and center.

Maybe you already know this, but just in case some piece of advice:

If you are using `parmap.map` in a loop like this:

```python
import parmap

for i in range(1000):
    par_list = create_list()
    par1 = create_par1()
    par2 = create_par2()
    parmap.map(func, par_list, par1, par2)
```
`parmap.map` will start (and close) subprocesses 1000 times. (On my laptop) the overhead of starting the subprocesses is of 0.07 seconds for each `parmap.map` call (so 0.07*1000 = 70 extra seconds in total in this case). This may be small compared to the cost of evaluating func, but sometimes it is worth to take care of creating and closing the multiprocessing pool only once outside the loop as follows:

```python
import multiprocessing
import parmap

try:
    pool = multiprocessing.Pool()
    for i in range(1000):
        par_list = create_list()
        par1 = create_par1()
        par2 = create_par2()
        parmap.map(func, par_list, par1, par2, pool=pool)
finally:
    pool.close()
    pool.join()
```

Unless you are going to benefit significantly, I would suggest not to make the code more complex. But if you see your computer struggles creating and deleting python subprocesses, then it may be a good idea to do that.

HI Ilya
RFC3062 doesn't specify how the userPassword should be encrypted. That's something related to the server implementation and obviously evolves with the proceeding of decrypting techinques.

If you use extend.modify_password() without any hash algorithm you follow the original RFC3062 behaviour, but servers usually expect an hashed password, so I have hadded the ``hash_algorithm`` parameter to be current with servers implementation.
 
For openldap have a look at http://www.openldap.org/faq/data/cache/1347.html and point 14.4 of http://www.openldap.org/doc/admin24/security.html. Other servers can have different usage or do not accept at all the ModifyPassword() extended operation.

Bye,
Giovanni

I just remembered that visual studio doesn't output any linkable symbols by default.  So it basically doesn't generate a shared library.  You are supposed to put __declspec(dllexport) in front of every function and class to tell visual studio to export them.  But that is grotesque so I'm definitely not doing that.  Instead, I would suggest making the following changes to tell cmake to only output a static library when on windows (except for cygwin which is unix).  So add these changes:

--- a/dlib/CMakeLists.txt
+++ b/dlib/CMakeLists.txt
@@ -121,7 +121,7 @@ if (NOT TARGET dlib)

    if (DLIB_ISO_CPP_ONLY)
        add_library(dlib STATIC ${source_files} )
-       if (NOT DLIB_IN_PROJECT_BUILD)
+       if (UNIX AND NOT DLIB_IN_PROJECT_BUILD)
            add_library(dlib-shared SHARED ${source_files} )
        endif()
    else()
@@ -420,7 +420,7 @@ if (NOT TARGET dlib)
       add_library(dlib STATIC ${source_files} )
       target_link_libraries(dlib ${dlib_needed_libraries} )

-      if (NOT DLIB_IN_PROJECT_BUILD)
+      if (UNIX AND NOT DLIB_IN_PROJECT_BUILD)
           add_library(dlib-shared SHARED ${source_files} )
           target_link_libraries(dlib-shared ${dlib_needed_libraries} )
       endif()
@@ -429,14 +429,25 @@ if (NOT TARGET dlib)

    # Install the library
    if (NOT DLIB_IN_PROJECT_BUILD)
-       set_target_properties(dlib-shared PROPERTIES
-                                    OUTPUT_NAME dlib
-                                    VERSION ${VERSION})
-       install(TARGETS dlib dlib-shared
-               EXPORT dlib
-               LIBRARY DESTINATION lib
-               ARCHIVE DESTINATION lib)
-
+        if (UNIX)
+          set_target_properties(dlib-shared PROPERTIES
+                                OUTPUT_NAME dlib
+                                VERSION ${VERSION})
+          install(TARGETS dlib dlib-shared
+             EXPORT dlib
+             LIBRARY DESTINATION lib
+             ARCHIVE DESTINATION lib
+             RUNTIME DESTINATION bin
+             )
+       else()
+          install(TARGETS dlib
+             EXPORT dlib
+             LIBRARY DESTINATION lib
+             ARCHIVE DESTINATION lib
+             RUNTIME DESTINATION bin
+             )
+       endif()
+
        install(DIRECTORY ${CMAKE_SOURCE_DIR}/ DESTINATION include/dlib FILES_MATCHING PATTERN "*.h")

I was disappointed to learn by way of the above that

> Even gmsh demands one and only one tag to be present for each cell in both physical and geometrical groups.

but indeed, from Gmsh's [MSH ASCII file format](http://gmsh.info/doc/texinfo/gmsh.html#MSH-ASCII-file-format):

> By default, the first tag is the number of the physical entity to which the element belongs; the second is the number of the elementary geometrical entity to which the element belongs; the third is the number of mesh partitions to which the element belongs, followed by the partition ids (negative partition ids indicate ghost cells). A zero tag is equivalent to no tag. Gmsh and most codes using the MSH 2 format require at least the first two tags (physical and elementary tags). 

That's quite a shortcoming.  I wondered what happens if two physical entities are defined that overlap.  I tried this with:

```
Point (1) = {0, 0, 0, 1};
Point (2) = {1, 0, 0, 1};
Point (3) = {2, 0, 0, 1};

Line (1) = {1, 2};
Line (2) = {2, 3};

Physical Line("left", 1) = {1};
Physical Line("all", 2) = {1, 2};
```

and found that Gmsh makes two lots of elements over 0 < *x* < 1.

```
$MeshFormat
2.2 0 8
$EndMeshFormat
$PhysicalNames
2
1 1 "left"
1 2 "all"
$EndPhysicalNames
$Nodes
3
1 0 0 0
2 1 0 0
3 2 0 0
$EndNodes
$Elements
3
1 1 2 1 1 1 2
2 1 2 2 1 1 2
3 1 2 2 2 2 3
$EndElements
```

The first two elements here have the same endpoints.

I suppose in practice (and I'm very surprised that I haven't hit this yet in my work) that what one would have to do is define three separate physical entities for the intersection and two relative complements and then use the union of the intersection and one of the two relative complements.  Not nice.

In one of those places (probably nsone if others are working), we could add to that section of the plugin a check:

- If the record content is in the format `*.domain.tld`, query the value of TXT record for `_acme-challenge.domain.tld`
- If it exists, store value, delete the record, recreate as a multiple record
- If it doesn't exist, store value in a temporary variable
- If the record content is in the format `domain.tld`, query the value of TXT record for `_acme-challenge.domain.tld`
- If it exists, store value, delete the record, recreate as a multiple record
- If it doesn't exist, store value in a temporary variable

This series of logic would result in a two answer TXT record with no changes to Lexicon.

To clarify, the reason for adding TCP support here is to support our use-case of pulling messages containing events from an AMQP queue and forward them to statsd with delivery confirmation.  While UDP makes sense for initial collection, when using statsd like this as a component within a larger metric collection system we want the ability to tell whether the metric was received by statsd so that it can be retried if statsd is not available (using AMQP as a buffer).

If files are open/closed too fast for psutil there's nothing you can do. `open_files` tells you which files are open in a *precise moment in time*, similarly to any other function (cpu, memory, disk, etc) and that really is it. 
As for info about closed files: the method is called `open_files` because it returns files which are *opened*. Of course if a file is closed is closed. The kernel does not keep track of that (why should it?) so there's nothing you can do.

I certainly don't mind adding more examples to the README, but I definitely will not add a `square` function because it's very easy to compute the square of a quaternion `q` as `q*q`.  Of course, you seem to misunderstand what "square" means: in every group — no matter how abstract — the square of an element is just that element times itself, which is not the same as the element times its conjugate (for groups in which a conjugate even exists), which is what `norm` computes.

As for `real` and `imag`, these functions are already implemented as you had hoped for individual quaternion objects.  Unfortunately, due to limitations of numpy, it appears that those can't be made to work for quaternion arrays without breaking them for other things — notably complex arrays.  I am keeping an eye on this problem.  For now, for the fastest way to get the respective parts, I recommend code like this:
```python
q_real = quaternion.as_float_array(q)[:, 0]
q_imag = quaternion.as_float_array(q)[:, 1:]
```

> result = handle(), bot.message_loop(result)

no , not exactly. did you read on the links i provided ?

there is a difference between
```python
bot.message_loop(handle())
```
and
```python
bot.message_loop(handle)
```
what you wrote in two statements is the first one.

the first one executes the handle and sends it results to the `message_loop` however, the second one doesn't execute the `handle`. it sends the function itself, thus allowing the message_loop to execute the `handle` when a new message is sent to the bot. the `msg` object is given to the handle inside the message loop.

this is a simple implantation of `message_loop`, the real one basically does this but with some more code for error checking and routing and other stuff.
```python
def message_loop(handle): # handle is the function handle itself, not its result
    offset = 0 # this is to prevent getting duplicate messages
    while True:
        # this is just a wrapper for telegram api, gets new messages
        messages = bot.getUpdates(offset=offset, timeout=10)
        if len(messages) > 0: # check if bot has recieved new message(s)
            offset = messages[-1]['update_id'] +1 #again to prevent duplicate messages
            for msg in messages: #get Updates can return multiple messages , so we need a for loop
                # this is the important part, handle function that is passed to message loop can 
                # be called from here, also here is where msg comes from
                handle(msg)
```

I ran into this too. For anyone who can't wait for a fix, I wrote a drop-in alternative that's much faster (2 orders of magnitude on my machine) but only supports a subset of what freezegun does: https://github.com/simon-weber/python-libfaketime.

Some ideas on making freezegun faster:
* make the new behavior optional:
  * easiest fix I can think of
  * doesn't fix the problem for people who need aliased module support, though having folks find-and-replace is probably easier than adding freezegun support :grin: 
* [abuse the garbage collector](https://docs.python.org/2/library/gc.html#gc.get_referrers) to make the search for aliased modules faster: 
  * potentially a quick fix
  * I'm not sure if a) it'd work, or b) it'd even be much faster (since python probably doesn't keep a reverse index for all objects?)
  * pretty ridiculous
* change the replacement logic to install patches once, then leave them in place and dynamically turn them on and off:
  * probably the most robust solution, but might take some time to implement
  * introduces some overhead on non-mocked date/time calls. https://pypi.python.org/pypi/wrapt could alleviate some of that if needed
  * this is what https://github.com/venmo/btnamespace does ([code](https://github.com/venmo/btnamespace/blob/master/btnamespace/namespace.py))

I understand the relationship between swagger 2 and rest serializers isn't 1 to 1; but i'm suggesting this might not be a limiting factor.

For example, we know from the view if we are reading vs. writing (i.e. GET or POST). This information could be used to generate multiple swagger definitions for a single serialzier that make use of different interpretations of fields.

Take the following serializer and model:

```python
class Todo(models.Model):
    title = models.TextField(null=False)
    detail = models.TextField(null=True)
    owner = models.ForeignKey(User)
    created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)


class TodoSerialzier(models.Model):
    owner = serializers.PrimaryKeyRelatedField(default=serializers.CurrentUserDefault(), read_only=True)
    created_at = serializers.DateTimeField(read_only=True)

    class Meta:
        model = Todo
        fields = '__all__'
```

If I were to create a swagger spec for a `ModelViewSet` using this serializer I would probably write the following

```yaml
paths:
  /todos/:
    get:
      tags:
        - Todos
      summary: list Todos
      operationId: listTodos
      responses:
        200:
          description: a list of Todos
          schema:
            type: array
            items:
              $ref: '#/definitions/Todo'

    post:
      tags:
        - Todos
      summary: create a new Todo
      operationId: createTodo
      parameters:
        - name: Todo
          in: body
          description: the initial values for the Todo model
          schema:
            $ref: '#/definitions/postBodyDefinition'
      responses:
        201:
          description: the new Todo
          schema:
            $ref: '#/definitions/CreateTodo'

definitions:
  Todo:
    type: object
    required:
      - id
      - name
      - created_at
      - owner
    properties:
      id:
        type: number
      name:
        type: string
      detail:
        type: string
      created_at:
        type: string
      owner:
        type: number

  CreateTodo:
    type: object
    required:
      - name
      - detail
    properties:
      name:
        type: string
      detail:
        type: string
```

In reality, there is only 1 model and 1 serializer but the spec can include 2 different definitions. These definitions are generated by considering if the current view path is GET or POST (read or write) and then using different rules when creating the schema from the serializer. I.e. if the method is GET, then all `write_only` fields on the serializer should not be included, all model/serializer fields that are `null=False` should be a `required` field on the schema. Where as if the method is POST then different rules are used; `write_only` fields are included as `required` fields on the schema, serializer/model fields that have `null=False` are also required fields, ... etc.

Hopefully this explains my idea more clearly. The goal is to generate endpoint specific swagger definitions even if they derive from the same serializer.

The directional derivative will be the dot product of the gradient with the (unit normalized) vector. This is of course possible to do with numdifftools and you could do it like this:

    >>> import numpy as np
    >>> import numdifftools as nd
    >>> v = np.r_[1, -1]
    >>> v = v/np.linalg.norm(v)
    >>> x0 = [2, 3]
    >>> def rosen(x): return (1-x[0])**2 + 105.*(x[1]-x[0]**2)**2
    >>> directional_diff = np.dot(nd.Gradient(rosen)(x0), v)
    >>> directional_diff
    743.87633380824832

[(datetime.timedelta(0, 13260), datetime.timedelta(0), 'LMT'),

The core goal of Pyrebase is to closely map the standard Firebase API, so a service like geofire is outside the scope of this library.

This is a really old issue! But for anyone who might be searching for a solution to this problem, this is how I was able to authenticate using a service account with environment variables only:

```python
import os
import pyrebase

firebase = pyrebase.initialize_app({
    'apiKey': os.environ['FIREBASE_API_KEY'],
    'authDomain': os.environ['FIREBASE_AUTH_DOMAIN'],
    'databaseURL': os.environ['FIREBASE_DATABASE_URL'],
    'serviceAccount': {
        # Your "Service account ID," which looks like an email address.
        'client_email': os.environ['FIREBASE_CLIENT_EMAIL'], 
        # The part of your Firebase database URL before `firebaseio.com`. 
        # e.g. `fiery-flames-1234`
        'client_id': os.environ['FIREBASE_CLIENT_ID'],
        # The key itself, a long string with newlines, starting with 
        # `-----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----\n`
        'private_key': os.environ['FIREBASE_PRIVATE_KEY'].replace('\\n', '\n'),
        # Your service account "key ID." Mine is 40 alphanumeric characters.
        'private_key_id': os.environ['FIREBASE_PRIVATE_KEY_ID'],
        'type': 'service_account'
    },
    'storageBucket': ''
})
```

You'll see [here](https://github.com/thisbejim/Pyrebase/blob/2c152ae56f83bc653bf6204a3c9f68fa97b9d64a/pyrebase/pyrebase.py#L47-L50) that the `serviceAccount` config value is handled differently depending on if it is a `str` or `dict`.

AWS ECS, at least, is able to deal with environment variables of the length necessary to store the `FIREBASE_PRIVATE_KEY`.

There's a few things to note here:

* This PR treats one host as the "preferred" host, and uses the others only as a fallback. This is not a good approach to fail-over or load-balancing. In short, simple fail-over to backup hosts in `Connection.open()` doesn't properly solve any high availability or load balancing problems. Dedicated solutions such as haproxy do. See the comment and discussion referenced earlier. Maybe @chase-seibert can chime in here and explain?
* Happybase is only a consumer of the Thrift API and its exceptions; it shouldn't raise exceptions "owned" by other libraries (which this PR does), i.e. it touches another's "private parts".

Howdy. Yes, this is a totally transductive implementation. That is - you have a matrix `X` and all of our algorithms complete them based only on the present values in `X`. 

There is no support for inductive mode here. You have to separately complete `X_train` and `X_test`.

Here's another explanation of the pitch:
https://www.freetype.org/freetype2/docs/glyphs/glyphs-7.html

The way I understand it is that for just reading the buffer into a numpy array, num_bytes = rows * abs(pitch) should work correctly in all cases. If the pitch is negative the order of the rows has to be reversed (easy to do in numpy). Since the pitch is the number of bytes per row and width the number of pixels, for a normal grayscale (1 pixel = 1 byte) both are the same however if it's a black and white image  (1 pixel = 1 bit) you have to unpack the pixels. That should be equally easy on a numpy array.

I think it would make sense to include something that can be used directly with np.frombuffer into the library, maybe method number 4 or 5.

The remaining question is, should the user immediatly create a copy of the array? Since I am not sure how and when the memory of the buffer will be freed.

Let's cut some complexity out.  
There are really 3 different needs here:
- Caching file reads (use something like an LRU cache, embed it in the ContentHandler class)
- Schema caching.  If there are schemas, and they're static, pre-emptively read them in
- Parsing XML/JSON/whatever (each extractor has one type it uses).

So, instead we just have a parse_type for the extractors on body content, and each extractor call gets a parse_cache or something with {'json': 'parsed as json', 'xml': 'parsed as XML' etc etc} where merited?

On Wed, Apr 18, 2018 at 01:42:03PM -0700, Luan Fonseca wrote:
> >>> print(get_real_ip(request))

That's part of the deprecated v1 API [1].  The v2 API is get_client_ip
[2].

  from django.conf import settings
  from django.http import HttpRequest
  from ipware import get_client_ip

  settings.IPWARE_PRIVATE_IP_PREFIX = ()
  request = HttpRequest()
  request.META['REMOTE_ADDR'] = '127.0.0.1'
  get_client_ip(request)
  # ('127.0.0.1', False)

The False is because 127.0.0.1 is (appropriately, in the
global-network context) not routable.

[1]: https://github.com/un33k/django-ipware/blob/2.0.2/docs/README-VER1.md#django-ipware-deprecated
[2]: https://github.com/un33k/django-ipware/tree/2.0.2#how-to-use

This little Python script create a file ("john.xlsx") with a sheet "test". In this sheet I display three numbers and a sparkline. Those 3 numbers are of very similar size and yet the sparkline looks interesting as ymin=1.9 rather than 0.0 (as I tried to set).


import pandas as pd


if __name__ == '__main__':
    data = "john.xlsx"
    writer = pd.ExcelWriter(data, engine='xlsxwriter')

    a = pd.DataFrame(data=[1.9,2.0,2.2]).transpose()
    a.to_excel(writer, sheet_name="test")
    
    worksheet = writer.sheets["test"]
    wmax = 3.0
    wmin = 0.0
    worksheet.add_sparkline("E2", options={'range': 'B1:D1', 'type': 'column', 'max': wmax, 'min': wmin})
    writer.close()

Thanks for the report.

The short answer is that the string value needs to be doubly quoted, as in Excel, like this:
```python
worksheet.conditional_format('A1', {'type': 'cell', 
                                    'criteria': 'equal to', 
                                    'value': '"Test"', 
                                    'format': cell_format})
```

This is called out in the docs: https://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.io/working_with_conditional_formats.html?highlight=quoted

Longer answer is that since this is a frequently encountered issue I tried to introduce automatic quoting in version 1.0.7 (see #547). However this broke formula handling, see #564, so I reverted the change in 1.0.9.

I may reintroduce automatic quoting in a later release with some additional requirements on formulas but for now just quote the value string.

I will explain with an example, If a user asks **What is a cat** first we will tokenize it or we remove the stopwords and then tokenize it. Ater tokenizing we will get 4 tokens **What, is, a, cat** and then we will compare all this 4 tokens one by one to all the keys that we have in our json data because we don't know what is the keyword yet. Then after finding the key from the json data we will search for a closest match for the question from that particular node. Then we will get an accurate answer.

_forms.py:190_

if ca and expires and ca.expires.date() < expires:
    ...
```
Changing sign_cert to use the same logic as forms seems like the easiest method to fix.

I suppose I could change the release workflow to be more like:

* push the (signed) tag to github
* download github's tarball
* compare the contents to what I get from `sdist`
* sign that tarball
* upload everything to pypi

OK I can see the argument about “auto” being unclear and I really don’t insist on it, it was just the best thing I saw so far.

I cannot follow the data argument though.  Yes it’s about data, but *everything* is data in the end, including code (let’s not have a lisp conversation tho :)).  And every class should carry some data in the end, otherwise it’s rather a module…

So let’s take a step back an loop what it is about more specifically.

It’s about attributes and the dunder boilerplate involved, right? (which btw makes attrs a really good name for attrs IMHO. ;))

So even something colloquial and heavy handed like `@auto_dunders` that talks about **what is done** would be preferable to me than a `@dataclass` which ostensibly talks about **the class** which it IMHO shouldn’t.

We should always stress that the *class* at the end is a 100% regular class with added code that operates on the defined *attributes*.

Am I making any sense to y’all at all?

***

The comment is generated by `django.contrib.admin`, not django-reversion. I don't know why it chooses to create that erroneous comment.

Saving the model will always save a revision, even if nothing has changed. That's expected behaviour. Anything else generates race conditions in the database, and incurs a performance overhead.

A block of code has to be wrapped in a revision for revisions to be created
in the DB.

You can do this explicitly with reversion.create_revision(), or you can use
view decorators, view mixins, or middleware to do this automatically. A
model form by itself will not create a revision.

My understanding is that the pre_save signal won't allow you to get the state of the model before it was changed. At this point the model will have been saved, it just won't have been committed to the database yet.

Personally, I'd just build your code completely separate, and then make you own register_all() method that registers a model with both reversion and your own code. Complete separation will make it easier for you to upgrade to future versions of django-reversion.

I've got some questions about how this works. Maybe I'm missing something. I've never actually implemented a multi-db app!

1. The reversion accessors (get_for_object, etc) default to looking for revisions in the same database as the object originated from, yet the reversion context managers will use the default database until explicitly specified. This seems like inconsistent behaviour.

2. The method of specifying the database to use in the revision context manager will break if multiple context managers are nested, since reversion only saves a revision when the outermost context manager exits.

```python
with reversion.create_revision(using="foo"):
    with reversion.create_revision(using="bar"):
        obj.save()
```

The above code will save the new revision into "foo". In a more complex example, where the nested context managers are actually in very different parts of the code, this could get dangerous.

My suggestions would be:

1. Either default to saving versions into the same database as the object on write (but what if there are objects within the same revision using different databases?), or make choice of database a completely manual process in all cases (ie. stay out of obj._state).

2. Throw a RevisionManagementError if a nested context manager is attempting to use a different DB to the outer one.

I've found out the observer should be set to an attribute, and then it works:

```python
"""Test observer helpers."""
from sqlalchemy_utils import observes
import sqlalchemy as sa
engine = sa.create_engine('sqlite:///:memory:')
from sqlalchemy.ext.declarative import declarative_base
Base = declarative_base()
from sqlalchemy.orm import sessionmaker
Session = sessionmaker(bind=engine)
import time


class Director(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'director'
    id = sa.Column(sa.Integer, primary_key=True)
    name = sa.Column(sa.String)
    modified = sa.Column(sa.TIMESTAMP, server_default=sa.func.now(), onupdate=sa.func.current_timestamp())


class Movie(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'movie'
    id = sa.Column(sa.Integer, primary_key=True)
    name = sa.Column(sa.String)
    director_id = sa.Column(sa.Integer, sa.ForeignKey(Director.id))
    director = sa.orm.relationship(Director, backref='movies')
    modified = sa.Column(sa.TIMESTAMP, server_default=sa.func.now(), onupdate=sa.func.current_timestamp())

    @observes('director.modified')
    def director_observer(self, modified):
        self.modified = sa.func.current_timestamp()

Base.metadata.create_all(engine)


def test_observer():
    """Test observer."""
    session = Session()
    director = Director(name='director')
    movie = Movie(name='movie', director=director)
    session.add(movie)
    session.commit()
    modified = movie.modified
    director.name = 'changed'
    time.sleep(1)
    session.commit()
    session.refresh(movie)
    assert movie.modified > modified

```

class MessageHandler(object):
    """Generic class to handle any messages that come through
    
    """
    def __getattr__(self, name):
        """Method to handle unknonwn method calls.
        
        :param name: name of function called
        :return: 
        """
        def method(*args):
            print("tried to handle method " + name)
            if args:
                print("it had arguments: " + str(args))
        return method

Hello,

This is the last step in the inheritance scheme indeed. At the moment, hvad supports inheritance with abstract models and proxy models. Concrete inheritance is not yet supported (unless the only translatable model is the last of the inheritance chain).

I think this is the first time someone requests it, because usually this kind of pattern is better implemented using Django's abstract models. (adding `abstract = True` to your `BaseBook`). Concrete inheritance leads to very complex queries on the database side, which would get even more complex with translations.

Despite that, I think we should add it, at least so implementation of Django inheritance schemes is complete. I'm adding it to the todo list, but I cannot give you a date right now. In the meantime, may I suggest you check whether your use case could leverage abstract models instead?

Dear @gordol you are right, using soket.io client in the worker thread is possible solution which is much better than one with `sleep` method invocation.

But comparing to `tpool`-based approach  I was not sure which one is better: using Socket.IO client from the working thread or using eventlet's `tpool` to start "signal waiting" thread. The former will use sockets for inner-process communication which is strange; the latter requires using dummy threads to wait for signal, using thread synchronization primitives and direct work with eventlet API. In terms of scalability and uniformity the solution to use Socket.IO client in the working thread is clearly better, I though about it before, but now, after discussing this issue with you, I think it is a way to go with as little kludges in the code as possible. Thank you all for this meaningful dialogue.

And, I think methods starting with an ``_`` are not necessarily meant to be only used from inside the object. I always interpreted them as something that is not part of the official API and thus should not be used from outside the library by users.

http://stackoverflow.com/questions/1301346/the-meaning-of-a-single-and-a-double-underscore-before-an-object-name-in-python
> _single_leading_underscore: weak "internal use" indicator. E.g. from M import * does not import objects whose name starts with an underscore.

@axt what you described is the intended behavior. `function_starts` is used as _extra_ starts besides the start points that `CFGFast` gets, which always include the entry point of the project. As there is no easy way to prevent CFGFast from using the entry point as one of the start points, I just added a new option `start_at_entry`. You don't need to provide the `SSECustomerKeyMD5`.  From our documentation http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/s3.html#S3.Client.put_object:

> SSECustomerKeyMD5 (string) -- Specifies the 128-bit MD5 digest of the encryption key according to RFC 1321. Amazon S3 uses this header for a message integrity check to ensure the encryption key was transmitted without error. Please note that this parameter is automatically populated if it is not provided. Including this parameter is not required

So:

```
s3.put_object(Bucket=BUCKET, Key=KEY, Body=b'foobar', SSECustomerKey=KEY, SSECustomerAlgorithm='AES256')

```

Thanks for your investigation. I am adding some minor comments based on your report.

Boto3's `Bucket.object_versions` resource collection to list all versions of an object. That interface returns `ObjectVersion` instances for both `Versions` and `DeleteMarkers`, but there is no property on `ObjectVersion` to indicate whether the instance represents a `DeleteMarker` or a `Version`. It turns out that the only way to identify an `ObjectVersion` as a `DeleteMarker` is to check if `Size` or `ETag` **or `StorageClass`**  is ~~`None` (assuming there is no other case when those attributes can be `None`)~~ **absent**.

> It looks like boto3 relies on the shapes defined in botocore for its resource types, so I suppose it is not possible to define a better resource type for ObjectVersion that has an `is_delete_marker` property, which the resource collection could populate?

It seems so.

> As far as I can tell, the only clean way of identifying delete markers in boto3 is to use the `list_object_versions` paginator, handling the `DeleteMarkers` and `Versions` attributes oneself. Is that right?

That is also a viable option.

To proactively address questions that will probably arise, the config package was a significant pain point prior to this refactor.  It contained a model for configuration data, an interface for (potentially) multiple implementations of a config backend, and one implementation of that based on etcd.  Having everything in one package like that made it tricky to code to the interface, agnostic of the implementation and made it difficult to create alternate implementations (e.g. an in-memory implementation intended to aid in testing).  The refactor was aimed at breaking that package up cleanly to allow better reuse and testing.

I just ran into this. The problem is the conda caffe2 package is built against libprotobuf version x and the onnx conda package from the conda-forge channel seems to be build against libprotobuf y. This leads to undefined symbols, or even segfaults when importing both onnx and caffe in the same Python application.

My workaround is to install caffe2 from the caffe2 channel with conda and compile onnx against the libraries from the conda caffe2 environment. This makes sure onnx is compiled against the same libprotobuf version caffe2 was compiled against.

Something along the lines of

```
conda install -c caffe2 caffe2

git clone https://github.com/onnx/onnx.git
cd onnx
git checkout vX.Y.Z
git submodule update --init --recursive
python setup.py install
```

Replace X.Y.Z with the latest version tag.

Note: compiling onnx requires additional dependencies (such as cmake, make).

@vlcinsky

- **setup.py** - Still not sure I understand the problem.  You run `pipenv install -e .` **once** so that setup.py is now tracked via your lockfile.  And then run `pipenv install` whenever you add new packages to setup.py.
- **Developers forgetting to update lockfile** - `pipenv --deploy` is designed to catch this.  Run it in your CI!
- **Pipenv fails** - agreed, if there are bugs in the tool, that sucks.  But bugs generally get fixed.  That's not a reason to throw away an entire philosophy 😞 
- **Tox**
  - If Tox is good for managing tests, that's great.  If it's also great for managing packages and **deterministic** builds, that's even better.
  - But if that were true, there wouldn't be a reason for Pipenv to exist. So I can only assume there's some kind of limitation with Tox.
  - Similarly to above, the state of the world **today** (the lack of good interop) doesn't sound like a reason to reject the philosophy.
- **Multiple envs**
  - It's clear that there are at least some special cases here, like Flask.
  - I don't have a better suggestion that multiple lockfiles (although maybe there's a future feature for Pipenv in this regard?)
  - But even in this case, I'm still not convinced that managing multiple lockfiles is an issue in practice.  Worst case, it seems that you could create a simple script `update-all-lockfiles.sh` locally, and run `pipenv --deploy` on your CI to catch errors.

--- 

@Moritz90 - Agreed, the "two phase" approach may be overkill in most cases.  In particular, if you're making deliberate/intentional "manual" updates to your lockfile, it's completely unnecessary.

--- 

More generally, it would be good to ensure that this "proposal" is reduced to the things that are actually hard problems (in my view, that's (A) serving multiple envs, (B) wanting to catch changes in upstream dependencies).  It shouldn't discuss transient things (bugs in Pipenv) or potential misunderstandings of how the tool is intended to be used.

That's kind of the problem, what do you mean "if it's up to date"? I don't want to lock my dependencies when developing, push that to dev, test and push to production, only to find that there's a new, incompatible version of some package, and the production server arbitrarily decided to install that one.

> So have anyone else thought about this problem?

Luckily, it turns out that Redhat have thought about this quite a bit!

The default compiler on the manylinux docker image is (a third-party rebuild of) the Redhat "developer toolset", which is a version of gcc 4.8 that's been modified to produce binaries that run against libstdc++.6.0.8 or later; so they work on RH/CentOS 5 and anything newer than that (which is basically everything).

(I'm not sure what these 3.4.* version numbers are?)

The way this works internally is some sort of arcane hackage where the linker decides for each symbol whether it's an old symbol that's available everywhere so it can be dynamically linked, or a new symbol that isn't available everywhere in which case it statically links that symbol. It's possible that this trickiness might cause some problems in some edge cases, but it's definitely what I'd try first.

@anarcat looks like I misspoke. I use package_data. You have to some stuff in setup.py and you also have to include the required files in your MANIFEST.in (to insure it's included if someone installs with pip)

Here's an example where I ship the data in the package folder: https://github.com/ninjaaron/lazydots for that, you just use include_package_data=True in setup.py

Thanks for the contrib.

I'm a little reluctant to add this for a few reasons. 

- It's copy/paste of code in distutils. 
- It's inline with other behavior, so it adds complexity and further complicates the distinction between the distutils and setuptools implementations.
- There are no tests capturing this requirement.
- This code seems to be explicitly excluded, probably because data files was deprecated in setuptools due to its incompatibility with encapsulated packages.
- This patch possibly runs in conflict with some pending pull requests to optimize the file list generation.
- It's encouraging use of MANIFEST.in, which demands that packagers dual-maintain the manifest of files (in the SCM repository and in another file in that repository).

That said, let's work toward a better solution.

I'd feel a lot better if distutils were to extract this functionality into a (perhaps private) method, and then Setuptools could call this private method (if it exists) or call a copy of it in the codebase for forward compatibility. As this requires committing to the Python codebase, perhaps that's too high a bar.

At the very least, I'd like for the code to clearly indicate why the setuptools sdist overrides the distutils sdist, and what it's intended to do... and to have a test to capture this new expectation.

What do you think?

The current implementation has 3 problems from my point of view:

1. You put a huge amount packages in the same repo. I get that this approach has some advantages, but is exactly the opposite of git and python best practices and common practices
2. Every single package has it's own release cycle (and therefore version number), so the azure one, is not a real meta package but an installer.
3. If this is an SDK, should have a _single_ release cycle and release number, otherwise is a collection of libraries (which is not the same thing as an SDK)

Also, the point of "many libraries" is a problem during the packaging effort because for many distro (included Fedora, and *EL, for which I'm looking at this SDK) every single pypi package has to be managed in a different (rpm) package, and therefore this means that to maintain the Azure SDK and Azure CLI I will have to maintain ~50 packages. On the other side, I'm the mainter of the AWS python SDK and AWS CLI tool and those are only 3 packages (2 for the SDK, 1 for CLI).

For instance we might generate a new configuration file and run migration whereas the user actually forget to set its configuration file path or didn't run the kinto command from the proper repository.

Also letting the user run: ``kinto --ini config/kinto.ini init migrate start`` seems to make a lot of sense for me.

The problem seems to be caused by a transform in pylint-django that substitutes a Manager by something that returns a list for all the methods that return QuerySets: all(), filter(), etc. This doesn't allow for the same methods on the QuerySet itself, so it doesn't recognise chaining, e.g. MyModel.objects.filter(...).select_related(...).order_by(...) 
If you replace transforms/transforms/django_db_models.py by the attached version, the errors disappear. I don’t know enough about pylint and pylint-django to know if this is an optimal or correct solution, but it seems to work for me. Maybe a test case for chaining such as the example above should be added?
Note that as well as the attached django_db_models.py, I also added ‘QuerySet’ to the _add_transform(‘django.db.models’,’Model’,’Manager’) call in transforms/__init__py
I hope this is helpful.

@yousifKashef Yes, Turi Create allows multiple bounding boxes per image. See the [user guide chapter](https://apple.github.io/turicreate/docs/userguide/object_detection/#data-acquisition) for object detection. The format for bounding boxes is a JSON object for a single bounding box, or a JSON array of multiple JSON objects for multiple bounding boxes.

There is no reason to limit to one bounding box per image; you can have as many bounding boxes as the number of objects in the image. I would recommend including a bounding box for all objects in the image, that are of a class that is labeled with one of the labels you are trying to predict.

That's correct behavior for the use of features, but I'm discouraging use of features.  What you could/should do instead is:

* clangdev package that has a dependency on a _clangdev_mutex-*-flang metapackage.  May have a track_features entry to make it not the default when someone does ``conda install clangdev`` (without explicitly or implicitly specifying a _clangdev_mutex dependency).
* flang package depends on clangdev AND on _clangdev_mutex-*-flang.  It does not have a track_features, because it has no competing installation candidates - there are no flang variants.

As long as every clangdev package has a _clangdev_mutex package dependency of some sort, this scheme will work.  If any clangdev package is missing that dependency, conda is free to choose an incompatible older package that is less constrained.  That's why hotfixing or replacing packages is so important here.
Yes, the two would be seen by conda the same way. `<pip>` is essentially packages coming from `pip list `.

> If a dependency has a track_feature then there should be no need to copy it.

I probably didn't make myself clear. Suppose there is an openblas package whose `meta.yaml` reads
```
build:
  track_features: - openblas_0_2
```
Then a numpy recipe might specify:
```
requirements:
   build:
     - openblas
```
and conda-build choses to resolve the dependency into the openblas package above. The thus created numpy package should now contain `features: - openblas_0_2` because it is probably binary incompatible with other BLAS implementations. Instead of relying on numpy's recipe authors to add this `features` specification manually, the proposed `features: - auto` would take care of turning a `track-features: - a_feature` in the dependency into a `features: - a_feature` spec in the dependent automatically. Does this make sense?

> When creating a .pmml file on a sklearn/xgboost model, does `n_jobs` or `nthread` carry over?

Model training and model deployment are separate concerns, and their "runtime preferences" are typically not shareable.

PMML is about representing the prediction algorithm. Different PMML engines may choose to devise a different execution plan for the same PMML document (eg. a naive PMML engine does all computations serially, whereas a smart PMML engine may do some/all computations in parallel).

BWT, if you're currently working on SkLearn/XGBoost, then you might want to check out the following announcement: https://groups.google.com/d/msg/jpmml/k6eWMXp8kAQ/xmfmb5wbAQAJ

I'm taking a little breather here, so I may be quiet for a while. I'll make one last point for consideration on this topic. I am in good agreement with many that the base-level functionality in functions is a key foundation for pvlib. When it comes to a "developer API" for adding DC model functions, etc. into `ModelChain` or `PVSystem`, it could be a valuable design goal to make this the same as the "user API" for adding these model functions (e.g., https://pvlib-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/modelchain.html#User-defined-models). I have seen several software projects where the two API's are different, meaning both have to be understood and maintained and usually one ends up worse off than the other.

> Solution 1 uses an explicit index, while solution 2 doesn't. Why? 

The explicit index in solution 1 speeds up the index join for the existing DataFrame due to something about pandas internal workings. No explicit index is necessary for solution 2 because pandas already needs to compute the join between the dict values.

> Also, solution 2 selects columns as a reflection in python airmass = airmass[['airmass_relative', 'airmass_absolute']]. Is that mandatory?

The assignment pattern in the existing code guarantees order. This line in solution 2 is necessary to guarantee that the column order is the same on all python/pandas version combinations. I hesitate to say it's mandatory, but we try to keep order consistent.

> I think the Sandia National Laboratories documentation is wrong on this one. Just imagine 100% circumsolar diffuse: tilted diffuse could never be greater than horizontal diffuse with this formula, even with the array pointed at the sun.

@adriesse you are correct, thanks for catching that.  We wrote `cos(AOI)` where we should have written `Rb`.  And we should give the equation for `Rb = cos(AOI) / cos(zenith).`

Loutzenhiser doesn't give an explicit expression for `Rb`. I could dig up the original Hay and Davies paper if we need to confirm, but others have written out the expression `Rb = cos(AOI) / cos(zenith)`

https://www.researchgate.net/publication/275276907_Comparative_study_of_isotropic_and_anisotropic_sky_models_to_estimate_solar_radiation_incident_on_tilted_surface_A_case_study_for_Bhopal_India

for example.

In your use-case, would it not be more sensible (from an API design perspective) to have an `/organizations` endpoint? Exposing database internals in an otherwise abstract API might not be a good idea if you want to change the underlying database at some point.

Note that you could always use regular Flask endpoints to add special endpoints behaving differently than Eve's default (which is CRUD).

I'm still not convinced this really is a feature we should add (and then basically have to support in all future versions). In the current stage it seems not too useful without additionally supporting things like `COUNT`, `SUM` etc. But doing that is _really_ database-dependant. Even selecting columns not in the `GROUP` statement and without an aggregation function depends on the database implementation AFAIK.

This sounds like more of an importer issue: assimp's API is that all tangents are `aiVector3D`, and it is assumed that they are all of the same handedness. (RH by default)

`glTF` allows you to use the `w` component to specify whether that particular tangent is LH or RH. I'd think that the importer should read that component, and based on its value, convert the tangent to whichever handedness is being used across this particular `aiMesh`.

Yes, you are correct. Daphne will try to send `ping` packets down the WebSocket every `ping_interval` number of seconds, and after the `ping_timeout` will close the socket and trigger a `websocket.disconnect` event.

The only case in which a `websocket.disconnect` is not triggered is if Daphne crashes, or if your server loses power.

All of the docker-py API is based around accessing the methods associated with the objects. How do I use the Container object down the stream in an another process, then? That goes with saying that there are ways to implement that using the docker-py API, but it's not documented.

Another point: urllib3 provides `__setstate__` and `__getstate__` for HTTPAdapter that is used to de/serialize the ConnectionPool used for accessing the API. So serialization does work if you use the S/HTTP, but not if using UnixAdapter.

```python
import docker
from multiprocessing import Manager, Pool

# This will work with https:// but not with unix://
ADDR = ''

def container_list_down_the_line(queue):
    cli = docker.DockerClient(base_url=ADDR)

    for c in cli.containers.list():
         queue.put(c)

def execute_in_subprocess(queue):
    while True:
        c = queue.get()
        c.remove()

manager = Manager()

queue = manager.Queue()

with Pool(2) as pool:
    res_1 = pool.apply_async(
                container_list_down_the_line,
                (
                    queue,
                )
            )
    res_2 = pool.apply_async(
                execute_in_subprocess,
                (
                    queue,
                )
            )
    res_1.get()
    res_2.get()

```

Hey @dave-nm, I think there's an error in the input.

`0101000020e61000003cdba337dcc351c06d37c1374d374840` does not contain a valid geometry type. (See https://github.com/geomet/geomet/blob/master/wkb_detail.md#point).

I think the problem is that you're missing a byte from the `type` field:
- the first byte (2 hex chars) is the endianness --> `01`
- the next 4 bytes are the geometry type --> `01000020`, which is not a valid geometry type (at least none that I'm aware of)
- the rest should be 8 byte chunks, each chunk containing a coordinate value

The first (working) example should convert to `{'type': 'Point', 'coordinates': [-71.060316, 48.432044]}`, but I don't understand what the other geometry is supposed to be. Can you please clarify?

As a little more background,  this is motivated by the fact that console_scripts and entry_points verify that the current Distribution (e.g. whatever is findable on the sys.path) has all required packages before executing.  These are defined by ```install_requires``` (and ```extra_requires```) in the call to setup(). If ```install_requires``` includes any pinned versions then installing a separate package version causes DistributionNotFound errors.

What this approach suggests is that requirements.txt defines the floor for all package versions. Developers (and importantly - plugins) can then install newer packages as needed.

@ajsierakowski for right now, you can do the following:
- register your client at auth.preview.globus.org/developers and make it as similar to your production client as possible
- set the `GLOBUS_SDK_ENVIRONMENT=preview` environment variable before running SDK code

That environment variable will tell the SDK to use the Preview Auth and Transfer APIs.

If you're having trouble, I'd also recommend:
- check that you're using the Client ID for your Preview client
- try turning on debug logging for the SDK (more on this below)

For debug logging, you need to use python logging. As a simple example,
```
import logging
l = logging.getLogger('globus_sdk')
l.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
l.addHandler(logging.StreamHandler())
```

Fair warning: we've made SDK debug logging _really_ chatty, but it includes details like
```
Request made to URL: https://transfer.api.preview.globus.org/v0.10/delete
```
which can be helpful when troubleshooting.

Something which I've found helpful in the past, and you may too, is to actually have your client code detect `GLOBUS_SDK_ENVIRONMENT` like so:
```
client_id = 'production_client_id'
if os.environ.get('GLOBUS_SDK_ENVIRONMENT') == 'preview':
    client_id = 'preview_client_id'
native_auth_client = NativeAppAuthClient(client_id)
```

Hope this all helps (and helps us write docs on this topic).

The reason for the failure is that  `py::str`'s check function permissively allows both `str` and `bytes` objects (or under Python 2: both `unicode` and `str` objects).  This is mainly here for Python 2 compatibility to allow `str` objects to be automatically converted to `unicode` objects.

We can easily add a `isinstance<str>` specialization does only checks against `str` (Py 3) or `unicode` (Py 2) to keep the up-converting behaviour and fix `isinstance`.  That's going to work as expected under Python 3, but the behaviour under Python 2 is going to be a little strange:

```C++
m.def("f", [](py::object o) { return py::isinstance<py::str>(o); });
```
will result in:
```Python
m.f("abc")
```
returning `True` under Python 3 but `False` under Python 2, which might be a little surprising.

I don't think it's necessarily *wrong* for `isinstance` to work that way; just perhaps surprising.

This works for me with Libre.fm, based on [bbcscrobbler](https://github.com/hugovk/bbcscrobbler/blob/master/bbcscrobbler.py#L215-L237) for Last.fm. If you've not done so, it asks you to authenticate via the browser, then saves the auth to a local file for next time:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
# encoding: utf-8

import pylast

import os
import time

LIBREFM_SESSION_KEY_FILE = os.path.join(os.path.expanduser("~"), ".session_key.librefm")


librefm = pylast.LibreFMNetwork(username='my_username',
                                password_hash=pylast.md5('my_password'))

def session(network, key_file):
    if not os.path.exists(key_file):
        skg = pylast.SessionKeyGenerator(network)
        url = skg.get_web_auth_url()

        print(
            "Please authorize the scrobbler "
            "to scrobble to your account: %s\n" % url)
        import webbrowser
        webbrowser.open(url)

        while True:
            try:
                session_key = skg.get_web_auth_session_key(url)
                fp = open(key_file, "w")
                fp.write(session_key)
                fp.close()
                break
            except pylast.WSError:
                time.sleep(1)
    else:
        session_key = open(key_file).read()

    network.session_key = session_key

session(librefm, LIBREFM_SESSION_KEY_FILE)

librefm.update_now_playing("Nirvana", "Lithium")
```

> Note that this is not the only possible solution, we could also go for `inv_std` completely and not use `T.nnet.batch_normalization()` any more. I just figured we shouldn't drop this possible advantage.

Implemented this now. Testing some variations, I found `T.nnet.batch_normalization()` is useful for the basic case only. As soon as you invert the standard deviation beforehands or set `beta=0` or `gamma=1` (constant, not trainable), it's too slow compared to the vanilla Theano implementation.
This being said, the basic case is probably still the one that's used the most. So now we have three variants:

* 8818d9c: The original batch normalization layer with `var` and `T.nnet.batch_normalization`
* 82103d4: An updated version with `inv_std` and `T.nnet.batch_normalization`
* de97c0a: An updated version with `inv_std` and no `T.nnet.batch_normalization`

I personally liked the first version best, but then we cannot become compatible with an upcoming `BatchNormDNNLayer` (if NVIDIA sticks to the interface of cuDNN v4 RC1). This would create problems down the road, making it difficult to save/load parameters between models that can and cannot use cuDNN -- we would need helper functions to convert the batch normalization parameters of a network from or to cuDNN.

By the way, when etags are used, I append its hash separately rather than hashing them together. This is because:
1. It avoids collisions for, e.g., `{"url"="http://allenai.org/1", "etag"=None}` and `{"url"="http://allenai.org/", "etag"="1"}`; this could be avoided by first inserting a character that's invalid in a url, like a newline or something, but this feels too hacky
2. If you have multiple versions with the same urls and different etags, you can see this just by looking at the filenames, because the url parts of the hashes will be the same

> how are you authenticating your DNS updates without signing them?

I run my own DNS server on a VPS. My web server and my DNS server are on a Wireguard VPN, both having static IPs. All packets on this VPN are signed. So, if a RFC2136 request comes from my web server's IP, the DNS server knows that it is legitimate. I use PowerDNS for my DNS server. PowerDNS lets me authenticate RFC2136 requests based on IP.

Here is a summary (mostly written for a Pycon-affiliated sprint, but applicable to all) if someone wants to get started working on this issue:

There's lots of investigating to be done for this issue. Almost all our unit tests currently pass with Python 3 (except `certbot-nginx`), but certbot doesn't actually work with Python 3. Ideas on where to start:


- run certbot with Python 3 with the staging environment, and try to fix it when it breaks
- run the integration tests with Python 3 and figure out how to fix them when they break
- look through the issues referenced above and try to figure out what’s wrong with them individually/how to fix them
- a fairly self-contained issue would be to try and make the nginx unit tests pass with Python 3, as they’re currently the only ones which don’t
- add Python 3 support for a specific subcommand (kind of like this: https://github.com/certbot/certbot/pull/3269) 

Here’s a guide on how to support versions both 2 and 3 simultaneously: https://docs.python.org/3/howto/pyporting.html (pay particular attention to the “text vs binary data” section, as that’s where most projects run into problems.)

As for getting setup with Python 3 using a development version of certbot, follow the steps here: https://certbot.eff.org/docs/contributing.html#getting-started, but replace `./tools/venv.sh` with `./tools/venv3.sh`. Then run: `pip install -e .[dev,docs] -e certbot-apache -e certbot-nginx`.

@bmw Good point about devs needing OS-packaged dependencies. We could probably get rid of Augeus, but we'll never get rid of openssl.

> Linux distros in general only have one version of a package installed a time.

In Debian, the convention is to add a number to the end of a library name when backward compatibility breaks: libfoo1, libfoo2. You can have 1 specific version of each major version installed at once.

So Debian's Python packaging subsystem, if it indeed not equipped to handle different major versions of a Python package at once, is a bit of a departure from how shared libs have long been handled. I expect this is due to Python's own deficient namespacing, where requests 1.0 and requests 2.0 are both imported as `requests`.

Having venv.sh use the le-auto pinnings seems like a reasonable alternative approach to me.

Update: this also contains a `--new-cert-name` flag to go along with a `rename` command. I'm starting to think that it may be appropriate to move verbs like `rename`, `certificates`, `update_symlinks`, and `revoke` to an interactive cert manager, but that is definitely not appropriate for this PR.

Once coverage and tests are happy I expect this to be ready for review.

I've taken a look at this ticket, PR #4047, tried to understand the current logic in `letsencrypt-auto.template`, and tried to think about use cases for plugins.

It seems like being able to nuke the `venv` is nice. Starting from a "clean slate" each time makes things easier to test, debug, and reason about. Less persistent state is, I think, preferable.

It also seems like a "one size fits all" solution for plugins may not be tractable, especially if we have a mix of first-party, third-party, and user-specific plugins.

Given this, I propose what I think would be a relatively small change:

Allow people to put scripts in a well-known location relative to the `certbot-auto` script (e.g., `certbot-auto.d/` or `certbot-auto-plugins/`). During phase2, between installing and running, run each script.

To be compatible with `certbot-auto`, plugin authors would simply need to define such a script to install the plugin into the `venv`. This could be anything from a call to `pip` to a command to copy files from elsewhere on the system into the `venv`, whatever is required for the plugin.

For consistency and simplicity, `certbot-auto` can ensure a set of a documented set of environment variables (e.g., `$VENV_BIN`) or even helper functions/aliases (e.g., `pip_install`) are defined when invoking the scripts. In this way, the work required of the plugin author is proportional to how special/unique their plugin's installation process is, without imposing restrictions on that process.

This process means that plugins behave similarly/follow a similar lifecycle to certbot itself when using `certbot-auto`.

This process also means that there is no point of contention when managing plugins; installing/uninstalling plugins is as simple as adding/removing files from a directory.

@jamiesmith the [`Cookie`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Cookie) request header doesn't define `httponly` or any other fields except for the key/value pairs. These are only available in the [`Set-Cookie`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Set-Cookie) response header. These two headers have a completely different syntax, and one cannot interchange them.

As such, I don't see what the agent could do when the wrong format is used in the `Cookie` header. We rely on the underlying framework (Flask/Werkzeug or Django) to decode the `Cookie` header, and even these two frameworks behave differently when presented with a `Set-Cookie` header:

    In [1]: from werkzeug.http import parse_cookie

    In [2]: parse_cookie("csrftoken=********; expires=Mon, 05-Nov-2018 12:35:24 GMT; Max-Age=31449600; Path=/; sessionid=********; expires=Tue, 07-Nov-2017 12:35:24 GMT; HttpOnly; Max-Age=86400; Path=/")
    Out[2]:
    {'HttpOnly; Max-Age': '86400',
     'csrftoken': '********',
     'sessionid': '********'}

    In [3]: from django.http.cookie import parse_cookie

    In [4]: parse_cookie("csrftoken=********; expires=Mon, 05-Nov-2018 12:35:24 GMT; Max-Age=31449600; Path=/; sessionid=********; expires=Tue, 07-Nov-2017 12:35:24 GMT; HttpOnly; Max-Age=86400; Path=/")
    Out[4]:
    {'': 'HttpOnly',
     'Max-Age': '86400',
     'Path': '/',
     'csrftoken': '********',
     'expires': 'Tue, 07-Nov-2017 12:35:24 GMT',
     'sessionid': '********'}

The connection count tracked by the API is actually how many *clients* are connected to the kernel via the notebook API. My thinking is that in some situations, you may not want to cull a kernel that has a browser tab open pointing at the associated notebook, even if the kernel is idle (e.g., kiosk mode). In others, you may want to cull regardless of whether there are connected clients or not (e.g., users at work forget they have tabs open). `cull_connected` is the knob to handle these two use cases. `cull_busy` handles the situation you described above.

At work, I plan to configure our JupyterHub instances to `cull_busy=False`, `cull_connected=True` such that users with long running jobs (e.g., Spark) won't lose work if they exceed the idle period, but browser tabs left open over the weekend won't keep kernels alive once they go idle.
FYI on blinking and accessibility: https://pages.18f.gov/accessibility/flashing/

As part of this, perhaps we add a `--conf-d` flag to the various config changers?

```
jupyter serverextension enable foo --py --sys-prefix --conf-d=foo
```
to create a `$PREFIX/etc/jupyter/jupyter_notebook_config.json.d/foo.json`?

Additionally, more generally, I'd love a high-level `config` subcommand that knew how to work with _any_ `Application`:
```
jupyter config nbconvert --sys-prefix --conf-d=foo \
  --NbConvertApp.executor_class=foo.KernelEnvExecutor [... more config]
```
or take a JSON file, I suppose.

This would remove the need for extension maintainers/packagers having to maintain things like `foo.enable`, where we have to use nasty business like `BaseJSONConfigLoader`, etc.

Additionally, `jupyter config <subcommand>` could show a pretty listing of exactly what would be used if the app was loaded, which would be really helpful for both supporting config issues, and creating/exporting reproducible configurations.

If I understand correctly the notebook is using RequireJS (e.g. https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/master/notebook/templates/page.html#L21) to load all JS modules.  This loads all JS asynchronously, but performs dependency resolution to ensure that scripts are loaded in the correct order based on their dependencies.  So for jQuery plugins for example it's going to be important that they're loaded *after* jQuery (though I would think you would get an error from the plugin if `jQuery` isn't found in the globals?)

In any case, because of this scripts embedded directly in the page with `<script>` tags will run synchronously, more or less as soon as the browser parses the HTML.

I've been lurking for only a few months on this project, mostly for grins and to learn a bit more about team collaboration on code projects, and over the course of that time I've seen the topic of multiple undo come up two, probably three times. In all circumstances, someone on the thread remarked how it's easy to accidentally screw up a notebook to where they can't get it back to where they want it. Since some people use this tool for Serious Work, they will naturally be upset if the tool lets them shoot themselves in the foot and doesn't provide a way to undo the damage. Those people might be prone to hyperbolic language when talking about their experience.

Having multiple undo would be one way to help mitigate the situation. There are more ways than one to skin this cat, though, and perhaps it would also be productive to talk about ways to help prevent the user from damaging their notebooks in the first place. Perhaps add barriers between the user and deleting input cells (individually, successively, or _en masse_).

What is _not_ productive is snapping at users about how much they should appreciate the work that's already been done. They'll either already appreciate it, or they won't. Dictating to them how much they should appreciate the developers and their work will probably not result in more appreciation. Instead, it is (in my opinion) more likely to result in fewer users, more of whom have a salty taste in their mouth.

For the record, this project is pretty awesome, and I'm amazed at the ongoing development and collaboration I see every day on it.

@jamiechoi1995 @MichaelLiang12, @TiRune is correct, inception_v3 has an aux branch, and if this is not disabled the forward function will return a tuple (see [here](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/7be960f50388ed06ec3d349841786e0a525640f2/torchvision/models/inception.py#L125)).

So you have two choices:
1) disable `aux_logits` when the model is created [here](https://github.com/pytorch/examples/blob/master/imagenet/main.py#L75) by also passing `aux_logits=False` to the `inception_v3` function.

2) edit your `train` function to accept and unpack the returned tuple [here](https://github.com/pytorch/examples/blob/master/imagenet/main.py#L194) to be something like:
```python
output, aux = model(input_var)
```

However, if you have to make a bz2 version for all readers I'm a little worried about clutter and keeping them up to date with any future changes. For a particular channel you can list multiple file types (as a list in YAML) and satpy will use the first file type that is available.

For the dask stuff, if the existing reader doesn't use it heavily then it may be more work than you want to do. I was thinking something like this:

```python
from dask import delayed
import dask.array as da
from satpy import CHUNK_SIZE

def func_using_open(...):
    return array_we_got_from_opening_the_file

delayed_open = delayed(func_using_open)
delayed_arr = da.from_delayed(delayed_open, shape=(rows, cols), dtype=np.float32, chunks=CHUNK_SIZE)

This assumes you can figure out the rows/cols and maybe the dtype from the header without having to load the entire file. I'm guessing that isn't exactly possible.
```

beat `cpptraj` speed with minial effort

just
```python
n_cores = 7
pt.pmap(n_cores, pt.radgyr, traj)
```

cpptraj
```
Read 30000 frames and processed 30000 frames.
TIME: Trajectory processing: 32.9545 s
TIME: Avg. throughput= 910.3449 frames / second.
```

pytraj: 6.6 s with 7 cores. About **5** times speed up. The scaling is not very impressive but I think it's acceptable. 

The point here is it's very easy to speed up.
Instead doing `pt.radgyr(traj)`, we change to `pt.pmap(n_cores=7, pt.radgyr, traj)`.

> I found the vcvarsall.bat script and and ran it with x64.

Okay. Given that you intend to use the Java bindings you should check that you have a 64-bit installation of Java (i.e. "windows x64" as listed at http://www.oracle.com/technetwork/java/javase/downloads/jdk8-downloads-2133151.html )

> When I ran the command:
cmake -G "Ninja" -DBUILD_JAVA_BINDINGS=ON
> 
> I got:
> CMake Error: The source directory "C:/Z3Prover/z3/build" does not appear to contain CMakeLists.txt

That's because your invocation of CMake is wrong. You need to tell it where the source tree is. CMake has no way of knowing where the source tree is because you can create the build directory anywhere on your system.

You should give it the path to the root of the Z3 git repository (i.e. the one that has on your system, e.g.

```
cmake -G "Ninja" -DBUILD_JAVA_BINDINGS=ON ../
```

This assumes that you created the build inside the git repository.

> Update: @delcypher I copied the example\c files into the build directory.

That's completely wrong. Don't do that.

To fix what you've done just delete the build directory you created and start again.

```
# I'm assuming you are in the root of the Z3 git repository on your system
rmdir /q /s build

# Now We start again
mkdir build
cd build
cmake -G "Ninja" -DBUILD_JAVA_BINDINGS=ON ../
# Assuming everything went right we can now build
ninja
```

A small example would be easier to understand, but I believe I see what the issue is: You define output bits using (define-fun ...) construct and inputs are (declare-fun ..).
(declare-fun ...) lets you create free variables while (define-fun ..) lets you create macros. The macros are expanded inside the parser. If you want to retain the macros as equalities, then you will have to use (declare-fun ...) followed by assertion of an equality.

          ; set_to true (equal)
          (define-fun |main::1::LO_Output!0@1#10| () (_ BitVec 32) (_ bv1082130432 32))

becomes

       (declare-fun |main::1::LO_Output!0@1#10| () (_ BitVec 32))
       (assert (= |main::1::LO_Output!0@1#10| (_ bv1082130432 32)))

To recover the output variables you would then have to walk the resulting formula.

I appreciate the reference. 
Is the application using the SAT backend or the SMT engine? 
They get compiled differently.

Kleiber and Kwon consider at-most-1 constraints using commander variables. 
It does not address at-most-k constraints, where I currently use 
bit-vector encodings for bit-blasting. This requires O(n log n) bits.
The encodings we use for a single bit is specialized to use a carry-bit, which leads to also an O(n) size, but different propagators. It is very possible that Klieber&Kwon provides better propagation. The SMT based backend uses Pseudo-Boolean inequalities that get compiled on demand to clauses.
