I was looking into this.

After messing around with objgraph I found an abnormal growth in memory (33 MiB to 500 MiB in a timespan of 12 hours). I originally thought it was my code but some inspection led me to this library instead.

Looking at the growth of object references over time I found the following:

```py
dict                       27731    +22390
deque                       9730     +8927
method                      9802     +8926
ServerDisconnectedError     4848     +4465
weakref                     6668     +4464
SSLProtocol                 4850     +4464
_SSLProtocolTransport       4850     +4464
ResponseHandler             4849     +4464
_SSLPipe                    4850     +4464
SSLObject                   4850     +4464
```

Which was odd given that I don't store references to any of those cases.

I began by investigating the ServerDisconnectError reference chain since I found it odd to store references to those, but that just led me back to `ResponseHandler`. I decided to print a reference graph to see where the backreferences lead to:

![](http://i.imgur.com/op4xAeo.png)

It ends at some list. I checked out the list and it's just a list of `_SSLProtocolTransport` objects that indefinitely grows. I am not sure where the source of this list is. I'm still investigating but I figured I'd post some progress and information here.

Apologies if this is not helpful.

The solution that I had in mind (and thought I wrote somewhere?) is:

* We store the initial balance of the contract as part of the computation state
* PAYGAS(n) requires both (i) the current balance is at least `tx.gas * n` and (ii) the initial balance is at least `tx.gas * n`
* If PAYGAS gets reverted, `tx.gas * n`  also gets subtracted from the balance.

I would design it as:

- a default middleware that only truncates `extraData` if the network ID in a whitelist matches
- an optional middleware that always truncates `extraData`

That way:
- If you are connecting to a compliant network, or a known PoA network, it will "just work" out of the box.
- If you have a custom chain on PoA that is not in the whitelist, you just add that optional middleware to your web3 instance.

`strsignal` allocates memory on some (all?) platforms and would potentially make things harder to diagnose in the case of heap corruption.  This is the main reason why I chose not to use strsignal.  Regarding your comment about relying on implementation details, `sys_siglist` is a documented part of the API and ABI on the systems that we support.

            "table": editor.quote_name(self._meta.db_table),
            "name": editor.quote_name(
                editor._create_index_name(self, columns, suffix="_uniq")),
            "columns": ", ".join(editor.quote_name(column)
                for column in columns),

To do what I said, you should look at raven.transports, as there's a 'send' mechanism. In your simplistic use case above, you'd simply write to disk on send, delete on success, and re-send queued on-disk objects when the transport is initialized.

I really can't say why, but intuitively I'm against changing the default behavior without a major reason. `raven.Client` does support equality, class instances compare by object identity by default. 

I'm not entirely sure what the general expectation is for equality in an object without an intrinsic value would be.  Choosing the arguments for the constructor method is somewhat arbitrary, as class instances can be modified later. Comparing __dict__ might be more sensible, but a complete implementation would include __eq__, __ne__, and __hash__, would break code that for any reason might depend on the default functionality (tbh can't think of a sensible use case either, but who knows), and would run into the same problem for any object referenced in __dict__ if we implement a strict comparison with `==`.

I'd say it's just easier to modify the tests to either assert identity, or subclass `raven.Client` for your tests if there is no other way. what do you think @dcramer ?

By "user side" I assume you mean outside of the Django Admin? Regrettably, we have not written `django-advanced-filters` with the intent of being a generic query building tool.

However, the basic idea can survive outside of the admin: the [`forms.py`][1] contains a (mostly) generic pair of forms/form-sets that can be used to create a "user-friendly" interface for simple query construction (notice that it currently expects either to be given `model_admin` or model `instance` on initialization, to determine the model).

Additionally, [the `AdvancedFilter` model][2] itself has only one job: it serializes and stores the query in a database record, so you could as easily use it for anything else.

So all that is left for you, is the following:
  1. Write a template which uses the form, for creation/editing of `AdvancedFilter`.
  2. Use the user selected filter in your views by getting the instance of `AdvancedFilter` and calling `instance.query` to receive back a Q() object to be used to filter the target model.

If you have any other questions, [hit me up on gitter][3].

[1]: https://github.com/modlinltd/django-advanced-filters/blob/develop/advanced_filters/forms.py
[2]: https://github.com/modlinltd/django-advanced-filters/blob/develop/advanced_filters/models.py#L16
[3]: https://gitter.im/modlinltd/django-advanced-filters?source=orgpage

While I was looking for the source of this particular test, I noticed that `List[int]` actually also results in a TypeFail, because `_handle_call` tries to call `__get_item__` with `int` as an argument. It doesn't result in any direct problems right now, because we usually use `List[int]` in type annotations and `_ann_node_to_type` has its own way of interpreting subscripts, and it ignores the `inf_type` set by `visit_subscript`. But it does mean that `List[int]` will show up in the final error report as a TypeFail.

To answer your question, I think the purpose of that code is to find cases where we don't want to call `__reduce__`, but whether it's any good is a different question.

The whole idea of jsonpickle is partially impossible, but hopefully we can evolve it to a point where it's at least on par with the regular pickle module.  When JP was first written, I didn't know very much about python or pickle protocols or any of that stuff, so it was written in a brute force, plow-through-it style.

Once example is how to handle circular references that can occur inside of tuples.  The whole proxy object thing falls flat on its face in light of things like immutable objects such as tuples.  But as a trick that works for a narrow range of valid objects, it's a neat hack.

I wonder if there's a way we could do without the whole proxy object hack for handling objects with back pointers and circular references.  The referencing thing is a challenge (and buggy in particular situations) because the assigned IDs are dependent on graph traversal order.  If we could somehow hash everything, maybe `id(...)` or something better, then we could fix that issue too by having a stable way to refer to objects.  The graph traversal order is why we have to sort the keys when traversing dictionaries.

It might also be necessary to traverse the graph and find the objects that are "leaf nodes" in the object graph.  Then we'd serialize those first, and then their parents, etc.  That would probably make it easier to handle object references.  Maybe `id(obj)` is good enough.  We'd then store all objects in a flat dict in the json output.  I wonder if we'd have to also store the traversed object IDs in the json file so that the decoder can know to restore objects in that same order.  Hmm.

Anyways, just some long ranty ideas I figured would be worth writing down in case it triggered any ideas.  Thanks again for the improvements, maybe these ideas are useful before settling on jsonpickle v1.0.  I've been really reluctant about that, heh.

Therefore systems that expect to call otLinkOutOfBandTransmitRequest, must implement otPlatRadioGetTransmitBuffer, such that it can provide multiple buffers.  Since the call to otLinkOutOfBandTransmitRequest and the implementation of otPlatRadioGetTransmitBuffer are both implemented by the 3rd party, this should not pose a problem.  Finally, we use this feature internally at Nest.

Example can be found in [testdata/simple](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/tree/master/tests/python/pants_test/backend/jvm/zinc/testdata/simple), absolute paths in [original analysis](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/tests/python/pants_test/backend/jvm/zinc/testdata/simple/simple.analysis) are replaced into [rebased analysis](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/tests/python/pants_test/backend/jvm/zinc/testdata/simple/simple.rebased.analysis).

There are two placeholders in the rebased analysis file
`_PANTS_WORKDIR_PLACEHOLDER` and
`_PANTS_BUILDROOT_PLACEHOLDER`, buildroot is `cwd` when zinc is called, workdir by default is under `buildroot/.pants.d` but can be anywhere.

A few options I can think of
1. zinc provides a mappings option so caller can specify arbitrary replace. This essentially moves rebase function into zinc and benefit all consumers. There is performance benefit too we today scan the entire analysis file (See rebase [implementation](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/d45d6eb47e3ad41a1e0f1469f70b9aa144cf22a5/src/python/pants/backend/jvm/zinc/zinc_analysis_parser.py#L126))
2. zinc w/o providing the generic option, make no assumptions of the absolute paths, always relatize wrt current directory. This is could be good enough in practice.
3. zinc still does what is today, just avoid wrapping them as java object, ascii works for us today, for example, the file hash in question can be just `path -> hash`

Since we already implemented rebase, this is first time path is encoded, I can workaround this time by decoding the base64, so this is not necessarily a blocker but a very much nice to have whichever we decide to implement.

Of course, but from the users point of view it's completely not what is expected.

For instance, if I want to have a default `dict` with values I must do this;

```python
foo = DictType(IntType, default=lambda: dict(eggs=1, sausage=5))
```

That seems a little odd to me and somewhat un-pythonic.

I would also question a situation where you'd actually want to share that default `dict` across instances of models, seems a very unlikely use case to me, if you want to share a variable across all instances there are much better alternatives that aren't going to cause the confusion that this approach will.

Would you consider a deep copy of the default on instantiation?

The undefined feature is now for the most part ready, but I'm having trouble deciding on the appropriate default behavior.

There will be two new import-stage options:

  * `apply_defaults` (described in detail in #360)
  * `init_values`, which controls whether to initialize values to `None` or leave them out (`Undefined`).

By default, neither gets an explicit value, in which case the effective values depend on `partial`:

  * `partial=False` implies `True` for both `apply_defaults` and `init_values`.
  * `partial=True` implies `False` for both `apply_defaults` and `init_values`.

To me, this seems pretty natural: if you want a full instance, every field will have a value, be it `None` or a field-specific default; if you want to work with partial data, nothing is set unless present in the input.

Here's the predicament: the current default for `Model.__init__()` is `partial=True`. Considering the above, I feel `partial=False` might be a more natural default.

Does anyone know if there's a particular reason `partial=True` is the default, or was it more or less a random choice?

If we retain `partial=True` as the default, doing `m = SomeModel()` would no longer set any values on the newly created instance, which can seem surprising. Accessing any field right away would raise a `MissingValueError`.

On the other hand, if the default is changed to `partial=False`, doing `m = SomeModel()` would populate all fields with something.

I'm tentatively in favor of changing the default. If we go that route, I think it would make sense for `import_loop` not to complain about required fields when no input data has been provided:

```python
class SomeModel(Model):
    f = StringType(required=True)
```
```python
>>> m = SomeModel()
>>>
```
```python
>>> m = SomeModel({})
  ...
schematics.exceptions.ModelConversionError: {'f': [u'This field is required.']}
```

Any comments?

Geodesic neighborhoods are correlated with nearest neighbors in particular respects, but quality neighborhood boundaries can obtained with geodesic neighborhoods by incorporating its neighbor step distance statistics.

Granted, "most" datasets do not really display anything more than globular manifolds in most embeddings, so walking across the manifold could be approximated with nearest neighbors. However, even with globular manifolds there is often some cluster separation that geodesic neighborhoods can detect, whereas nearest neighbors would bleed the selection beyond the confines of the natural cluster.

So natural cluster selection can be achieved with geodesic neighborhood selection, which would otherwise require fine-grained neighborhood size control if nearest neighbor selection were to achieve the same.

In having played around a lot with the different neighborhood selection options, I could offer from my subjective experience that geodesic neighborhood is definitely my favourite, especially for labeling samples. Just because it gives quality "cluster" selections without having to really fine-tune anything. Nearest neighbor often bleeds selections that violates the apparent manifold.

Of course, at face value, I would totally agree with you, keep things as simple as possible. Perhaps containing the geodesic neighborhood option in the dropdown helps to tone down the clutter. In terms of cluttering the code itself, we primarily have one extra KNNFunc. I am inclined to give opportunity to the community to provide feedback on the usefulness of geodesic neighborhoods.

The polymer component approach is a little more heavyweight, but the appeal to me was that then we can encapsulate the somewhat messy business of setting `inner-h-t-m-l=[[breakWords(escapeHtml(...))]]` so that it just takes care of the escaping and HTML insertion for you, with the ultimate goal of auditing and restricting usage of the inner-h-t-m-l hack so that as jart@ said we can be more confident about XSS protection for TensorBoards showing logfile data across trust boundaries.

Also, the interplay of word-breaking and escaping is also a little subtle; you can't escape after breakWords() since then you'd escape `<wbr>` itself, but if breakWords() was ever changed to break on e.g. `&` or `;` then you'd start inserting `<wbr>` into entity references incorrectly.  Making that process bulletproof would mean escaping each word segment individually before joining them with `<wbr>`, but at that point you could just use a "native" `dom-repeat` to do the join and avoid the `inner-h-t-m-l` usage entirely, which is an advantage you only get with a polymer component.

One suggestion of the Pip team was not to use `===` in the minimum constraints file, but to use `==` instead.

Using `===` is documented as "arbitrary equality" in PEP 440. Its documented purpose is to make sure  `1.0' is used when versions such as '1.0+localidentifier` are around. See https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0440/#local-version-identifiers.

There is some uncertainty as to whether `===` is really doing just a string comparison of the versions strings (so that `0030===30` would not match), versus still would be doing some amount of normalization.

But that is normal because when you use ```fa-ul``` class, it works by removing the default list style using ```list-style-type:none```. That means any CSS that will try to affect the list style will not have any effect, because it has been previously removed.

Now, I tried Chrome, no column samples works, it's a mess. The bullets are there, but looks like there are cover by a mask. Using Chrome debug tools, edit the property  ```left``` from the style rule ```fa-li``` to -1em and the bullets will start showing up under the mask. I will simple assume that is a wrong implementation of ```columns``` on WebKit browsers no ready for prime-time.

The Microsoft browsers look like are the only ones that have completed implemented all multi-column spec, but there are still some bugs. Look at the "Know issues" 6.

http://caniuse.com/#feat=multicolumn

Even that they are talking about ```ol``` is the same problem you are seeing on ```ul``` custom bullets. Firefox is OK, Edge just show the bullets on the first column, Chrome no bullets.

I trying to understand how the thing works, and I originally thought that ``_response`` is responsible for returning the validity of the domains. However, I made a mistake thinking that not returning the domain form ``_response`` means that it won't be set to boulder. Little did I know...

It seems to me that ``client.py`` handles the process of issuing a certificate. The problem lies in the fact that a CSR is created for all the domains. https://github.com/TheNavigat/letsencrypt/blob/4f3f2c5507395f1fab25bfb0d2f01f521c35cd0b/letsencrypt/client.py#L247-L266

The CSR then, including the domains, is sent to boulder here. https://github.com/TheNavigat/letsencrypt/blob/4f3f2c5507395f1fab25bfb0d2f01f521c35cd0b/letsencrypt/client.py#L198-L229

As shown above, the CSR is created before the domain is authorized. The result is that to prevent the domain from being sent to boulder (and thus prevent the error), we have to send a different CSR.

So, first choice, we recreate the CSR if there are domains failing.
Second choice, we issue a single CSR for each domain. Which is... yep, you guessed it! Insane.
Third choice, we restructure/recode/redesign ``client.py`` so that the CSR is generated after the domains are authorized and not the other way around.
And there's possibly a fourth choice I'm not considering.

I'm inclined towards the third option, because it sounds reasonable and because it shouldn't be too difficult. I'm not a CSR expert so I apologize if there are any mistakes caused by limited knowledge of how the whole certificate issuance process works, but it looks like CSRs are not used for authorization. A simple split of the functions, and calling authorization from ``obtain_certificate`` (and not ``_obtain_certificate``) should fix it.

> +ssl
> server ~ #

Ok, I've been making some potential progress this week. I have spent some time making a content agnostic listview. The code is stilll pretty hacked together, and not very delicate so I won't upload until its a bit cleaner. But since I can't work on this for a week or so, I'll write it up so I can get some feedback on it.

Here is the current user search view. (Plus some debugstrings i forgot to clean up).
![test](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1438488/25782225/ef6e6ef8-3346-11e7-9b99-15bdcdba78cc.gif)

I've been trying to work on a content agnostic list view and a few supporting functions and classes, reusing a lot of the generate_playlist_code. It differs a little bit from how PaginatedContent was expected to work as this view remembers its own page number. I also made it so that a function named _run() can be added to any content.Content derived classes which gets priority when matching userinput, so future uses of content.Content can hold its own functions. I use this to change pages. When you create a view, you can specify which function it is going to run when you've selected your candidate from the list.

I also made a wrapper class for call_gdata, that emulates a list of all search results, but it will only query for more pages when needed.

`parse()` converts timezone into `UTC` for the resultant `datetime` object unless specified otherwise. Think of this default choice as if you are collecting dates from a number of sources with different timezones, you'd want them all to be normalized in one timezone. Like you specified, it's configurable. You can achieve this in a couple ways. Either use `DateDataParser` like below or modify `settings` directly.

##### Using `DateDataParser`
```
In [1]: from dateparser.date import DateDataParser

In [2]: ddp = DateDataParser(settings={'RETURN_AS_TIMEZONE_AWARE': True, 'TIMEZONE': None})

In [4]: ddp.get_date_data('Wed, 11 May 2016 14:30:00 -0400')
Out[4]:
{'date_obj': datetime.datetime(2016, 5, 11, 14, 30, tzinfo=<StaticTzInfo 'UTC\-04:00'>),
 'period': u'day'}

In [5]: ddp.get_date_data('Wed, 11 May 2016 14:30:00 EST')
Out[5]:
{'date_obj': datetime.datetime(2016, 5, 11, 14, 30, tzinfo=<StaticTzInfo 'EST'>),
 'period': u'day'}

In [7]: ddp.get_date_data('Wed, 11 May 2016 14:30:00 EDT')
Out[7]:
{'date_obj': datetime.datetime(2016, 5, 11, 14, 30, tzinfo=<StaticTzInfo 'EDT'>),
 'period': u'day'}
```

##### Modifying `settings` directly
```
In [10]: from dateparser.conf import settings

In [11]: settings.TIMEZONE = None

In [12]: settings.RETURN_AS_TIMEZONE_AWARE = True

In [14]: from dateparser import parse

In [15]: parse('Wed, 11 May 2016, 15:30:00 PDT')
Out[15]: datetime.datetime(2016, 5, 11, 15, 30, tzinfo=<StaticTzInfo 'PDT'>)
```

Hi @kmike! thank you for the detailed description. `TIMEZONE` setting has been causing confusion to lots of users as it can be easily misconstrued for supplied date timezone. We never provided a mechanism to specify a default input timezone for dates. The only _relatively_ straightforward way to accomplish that is by appending the timezone next to date string and define the result timezone for the same.

```
parse('2016-09-07 12:30 UTC+5', settings={'RELATIVE_BASE': datetime(2016, 9, 7, 15, 0), 'TIMEZONE': 'UTC+5'})
datetime.datetime(2016, 9, 7, 12, 30)
```

But above doesn't work for relative dates and must be fixed for both reasons a) it's not straightforward b) it's not all inclusive.

I'll add/rename the settings. Will be pushing a fix soon.

FWIW when I first tried to get a PIMS object into Dask, I tried to use [`imread`]( https://github.com/dask/dask/blob/0.14.1/dask/array/image.py#L19 ). There were a couple issues I encountered.

First the `TiffStack_tifffile` was not picklable. ( https://github.com/soft-matter/pims/issues/265 ) Perhaps other things also suffer from this issue. Not sure if this is a bug or intentional. That said, Dask is going to want to pickle things. So having some kind of solution to this problem will be essential. Second it doesn't seem to be possible to do multiple slices at once. ( https://github.com/soft-matter/pims/issues/262 ) This needs to be handled somehow as Dask wants to treat things like NumPy arrays. The rest of the issues reduce to things that this PR is already addressing. Namely using the standard NumPy interface to allow for a consistent API.

Hi Thomas,

I looked into this further with the Juniper eng team and they see the problem here is because exa-bgp is encoding multiple instances of addpath cap, one for each family. So the last one received by junos is being remembered. 

Pls refer pkt #36 in the attached pcap in wireshark.

The spec mentions that an implementation should send only one instance of the capability: 

  A BGP speaker that wishes to indicate support for multiple AFI/SAFIs
  MUST do so by including the information in a single instance of the
  ADD-PATH Capability.

So it seems to be a bug on the exa-bgp side. 

When I disable inet-labeled addpath on exa, it sends only one instance of addpath-cap with inet-uni, and so it works.

The disconnect messages alone may not be much to concern yourself with. Librdkafka, and the clients derived from it, hold connections for each broker. This is true even if you are not producing to or consuming from that broker. As a result you will see these disconnect messages each time an idle connection is reaped. There is actually an FAQ for this on the librdkafka wiki which I have linked below: 

https://github.com/edenhill/librdkafka/wiki/FAQ#why-am-i-seeing-receive-failed-disconnected

@hugadams, thanks for joining the conversation!

Our goal is to reduce *our* dependencies, hence the move away from Qt.  Bokeh is not a dependency for mpld3, I meant that Bokeh would be a large dependency for us.  We already have matplotlib as a dependency, and I've been working on improving their webagg/nbagg event handling, which will be in their 1.4.3. release.  

See [datacursor](https://github.com/joferkington/mpldatacursor) for your contour picker question.

I agree that trading Qt for a moving target (IPython) is not necessarily ideal, but we've seen that requiring Qt can be a barrier to entry.  

TraitsUI is out because they do not yet support Python 3 (and the core devs have shown no indication that they are planning to any time soon).

We could also hand-roll our own UI using JQueryUI and websockets.  I think I can replicate what we have so far using that approach, but future growth would be limited by our lack of JS expertise.

Weighing all of the above, I think that Bokeh's growing stable of widgets and tools is attractive, and we would benefit from their (stable) growth.  As much as I like Matplotlib, I don't like being tied to the notebook and its changing interface.

According to https://www.w3.org/TR/SVG/text.html#WhiteSpace the SVG user agent will strip the space when processing the tspan node `<tspan> Text2</tspan>`.

Inkscape has it implemented just like that.

Firefox and Chrome basically ignore this is an SVG document but instead use rules similar to what would apply to HTML content.

As this will always cause inconsistent behavior there's no way we can ensure one or the other (unless we don't touch the content at all - which is not the idea of Scour after all). Therefore I decided to implement behavior that is at least compatible with the SVG spec (and Inkscape). Blame the Browser people for not following the spec, because really - what's the point of a spec if nobody follows it?

A persistent queue and invoking handlers via juju-run is problematic, because the hook environment that caused the particular states to be set and the handler queued will have changed by the time the handler is executed. The states will be making invalid claims about the environment.

I think the current model is the correct one, where the queue is constructed and drained in the hook context that triggered it.

The first problem cited that you are trying to solve is how to trigger the reactive loop from outside a hook context. juju-run seems the solution here, to create a hook context. A helper using juju-run could be installed by the base layer to make this easy for applications ('juju-run python3 -m charms.reactive.main').

The next problem I see is handlers being reinvoked even if their states have not changed. I consider this a feature, and this behaviour is currently being relied on to ensure handlers are invoked every time the hook environment changes. A `@hook` (or better yet, the proposed `@setup`) decorator is used to ensure the states are valid, and the relevant handlers invoked to ensure the unit is correctly setup based on the hook state and all the external dependencies, even if it has been previously invoked. As an example, a unit in a blocked state will want to keep reinvoking the handler that unblocks it until necessary conditions are met and the reactive state updated. If we change the default behaviour so that handers are not reinvoked if their declared states are unchanged, a new feature will be needed to support this and similar use cases. It would of course be backwards incompatible. A backwards compatible change would be a new decorator (or repurposing the somewhat broken `@only_once`) allowing people to explicitly declare what behaviour that need.

The next cited issue is handlers not being triggered immediately when state is changed by applications or actions. Using 'juju-run' to trigger the reactive loop seems the solution here for applications, per two paragraphs ago, and actions simply need to trigger the reactive loop themselves since they already have a viable hook context (eg. https://github.com/AdamIsrael/vnfproxy/blob/master/actions/start ).

I think `@hook` vs. states can be solved by not using `@hook`. There are other proposals for adjusting the ordering and firing of handlers that address the use cases for `@hook`, such as the `@setup` decorator to readjust the reactive environment before kicking off the main body of handlers. The apt, snap, leadership and coordinator layers all us a similar mechanism and do not use `@hook`.

The first approach in the proposal section seems incompatible with actions. Actions have a hook context, so the juju-run tasks kicked off by the persistent queue would block until the action exits. The second approach seems compatible with actions.

This proposal does not address how to handle charm upgrades. Again, the first approach proposed seems to fail here as Juju will be replacing the charm under the persistent queues feet. Inflight handlers will become invalid (as their locations in the .py files has changed, per `@only_once` problems), or if they are resident in RAM or .pyc then handlers will be trying to access invalid resources on disk such as templates. The second approach is workable, although a) all handlers will need to be given a unique id by the charm author or b) some magic that extracts the logical path to the handler and using that as the key (maybe extractable from the AST? I didn't have any luck last time I tried to tackle this).

In the larger picture, I don't think this gains us anything. I have not come across any problems with charms.reactive that I feel require a backwards incompatible 2.0 release and instead we should be moving forward the existing codebase. The existing design works well and has been pretty much universally adopted because of it.

To clarify, the goals of this are to:

* Simplify the reactive bus logic
* Simplify the external handler API and logic
* Better separate the handler conditions from their invocation, which should enable better introspectability during the build process
* Better enable applications deployed by charms to communicate back to the charm when the environment changes in a way that is not due to a Juju event
* More cleanly model the idea that handlers react to changes in flags, regardless of what causes them to change

> Although - it should still be possible to remove the nonce from each payment in the contract (and keep it for the UDP layer). 

The nonce is increased for each message, not for all payments, example:

- A -> B latest message M1 was with a transferred_amount of 10 and a nonce of 3, the locksroot is empty.
- N mediates a message through A -> B, the new latest message M2 from A -> B has a transferred_amount of 10 and a nonce of 4, the locksroot contains the hash of N's hash time lock.

Now let's suppose that B goes offline and was using two third parties, the first third party T1 lagged behind and only knows M1, the second third party T2 is up-to-date and knows the message M2.

If both T1 and T2 dispute on behalf of B the smart contract needs to decide which message is newer, the transferred amount is not sufficient (because it's equal), so we use the nonce to order the messages, and this is crucial because M2 has a pending lock, **if** the smart contract keeps M1 and the secret is revealed while the channel is being closed, B won't be able to claim the lock and A will keep the tokens.

We need the nonces to create a total order of the messages, because not all messages change the transferred amount.

As a second note, the nonce is not shared by design, if both participants of a channel update the nonce atomically synchronization among them is required, we separated sequential nonces and a monotonically increasing transferred amount we can have the same functionality without the synchronization (conflict resolution is done in a manner similar to CRDTs)

That's great that you updated it. The patch mentioned above is just part of a much larger file that also contains a class with keywords to extend Selenium2Library. That is why we do not get the no keywords warning. The important part is that the monkey patch class is loaded _before_ any instance of Selenium2Library is created.

As for your issue with `wait_until_element_contains`, that appears to be an issue specific to that keyword and `wait_until_element_does_not_contain`. These keywords perform the element search first and then wait for the text to change. Notice that the call to `_element_find` is outside the function passed to `_wait_until_no_error`. All the other wait keywords locate the element upon every poll (inside the function passed to `_wait_until_no_error`).

This is where it gets into a grey area. Some people would like to know when the AUT is replacing the elements in the DOM, and others would rather it "just work". Personally, I think it would be better to change these two keywords to (re) locate the element on every poll.

I think the solution I gave you in the previous comment will work.

In DRF test module, the `render` method of a`renderer` instance is being called [here](https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/blob/master/rest_framework/test.py#L65). As you can see, only `data` is passed to this method. There is no `renderer_context` which then raises an error when using `drf-json-api renderer`. I gave the `JSONRenderer` in DRF a quick look and it seems that it works fine if its `render` method isn't given a `renderer_context`. In fact, I think all renderers in DRF behave the same with regards to this aspect. Perhaps it was designed like this for testing purposes. They all have the following line in common:

`renderer_context = renderer_context or {}`

Anyway, if what I'm saying is correct, passing `content_type` directly to `post` leads to a different path where `renderer.render` is not called, see [here](https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/blob/master/rest_framework/test.py#L47-L49)

The tests written for the package either used the standard Django Unittest or pytest and some used the testing classes provided by DRF. For the latter, `content_type` was used when sending data.

As said during the meeting, another approach I would see is to keep `invenio-record` API and model the same way. The need seems to be that `additional error messages` should be provided in some cases.

These messages could be provided by having the serializers validate the record with an additional JSON-schema and dump the error messages in the response.

Pros:
- Flexible: this enables to have any kind of additional validation, or at least everything JSON-Schema enables.
- Simple: there is no need for a definition of what is allowed to be saved. Users just use the JSON-Schema format to define it. (This would also prevent from having additional error messages pop-up when the real publication schema is used to publish in invenio-deposit).
- Performance: In case a client application wants to do *live* check of data validation (while the user writes it), they can do it by just using the JSON-Schema on the client/browser. There is no need to submit the record to the backend on every keystroke.

Cons:
- This requires to have an additional JSON-schema or a way to generate one from the other.

Implementation suggestions:
- This could be standardized in `invenio-records-rest` serializers by adding a `warnings` field next to the `links` and `metadata` ones.
- There could be an additional field in the record pointing to the additional JSON-Schema (ex: '$warning_schema').
- JSON-Schema transformation helpers could be added to the doschema module. Example: one for removing all `require` fields except some specific ones.

It might be interesting to write a decorator that essentially turns an iterator into a request/reply system on widgets. For example, something like:

```python
@pause(widget, 'take_screenshot', 'screenshot')
def run_computation():
    for i in range(10):
        print(i)
        # do some computation
        screenshot = yield i
        print(screenshot)
run_computation()
```
which would register an observe handler for the widget. Every time the iterator yields, it sets the widget 'take_screenshot' value to i. When the frontend sees the value of 'take_screenshot' change, it takes a screenshot, which syncs the data back to the kernel. The observe handler for the screenshot attribute then calls the iterator `send()` method with the screenshot value, and you continue the function.

You need to have a `GlyphOrderAndAliasDB` file in the directory. That file needs to list all of the glyphs in the exact same order and with the exact same name as they appear in the source TrueType font.

`tx -t1 font.ttf > font.pfa`

On looking to this, I think it not appropriate to change current behavior. The real issue is that 'tx' operates on only Type1/Type 2 related information. It is not a OpenType reader/writer: it is a Type1/Type2/CFF font reader/writer.  It happens to work with OpenType fonts as input, but reads only the table, and can output only info from the CFF table.  The UFO font format contains a lot of meta data which is outside the set of data contained in a Type1/Type2 font - and is in fact, a superset of the OpenType data. I coudl certainly add support to carry through the fields openTypeOS2StrikeoutSize/ openTypeOS2StrikeoutPosition from a UFO font as input, but that really doesn't make sense all by itself - if we take 'tx' down this path, it should pass through all the meta data for both UFO and OTF, whcih is really outside its scope

So I played around with creating gzip assets as part of the webpack build process, but I ran into [an issue](https://github.com/cherrypy/cherrypy/issues/1558) with CherryPy. Well the GZipMiddleware is for the data served by Django, including the html file and other responses. And the gzip compression by CherryPy is for all static css and js files. So the compression by CherryPy would only occur on first request, and after that the browser would ideally just be referencing the cached assets.

> I was wondering how it ties into our previous thoughts about import/export commands? Maybe there's some terminology to keep an eye out for.. and maybe there's some modularity?

The discussions we had in planning this stuff definitely took inspiration from our previous discussions about the import/export commands. That's also why I renamed `retrievecontent` to `importcontent` in this PR, for consistency -- we have `importchannel`, `exportchannel`, `importcontent`, and `exportcontent`.

In terms of modularity, would love to hear specifically what you're thinking. One piece of modularity added in a previous PR and further used here is [`kolibri.content.utils.transfer`](https://github.com/jamalex/kolibri/blob/4715c99f3c4067c0425ad7d45ffde81e88df5f75/kolibri/content/utils/transfer.py#L26), which encapsulates async/streaming file transfer logic (both downloads and copying) into reusable classes/methods, so that we can add logic for error handling etc in a single place and take advantage everywhere.

I also previously added the [`kolibri.core.discovery`](https://github.com/jamalex/kolibri/tree/4715c99f3c4067c0425ad7d45ffde81e88df5f75/kolibri/core/discovery) app, which encapsulates the logic for *discovering* places to import/export to and from. Right now it just does filesystem stuff, but that's where the peer discovery stuff will go too.

> The ideas for import/export also encompassed user data.

Yep! Those pieces aren't written yet, and won't go under the content app, but will use similar terminology, and leverage the `discovery` app for identifying sources/destinations.

We can then have some higher-level import/export commands which call the various app-specific 
commands.

We added the `KOLIBRI_DATA` folder name, which is a standard name for an external data folder, e.g. on a USB key, and the structure of that folder directly mirrors the `.kolibri` (KOLIBRI_HOME) structure (/content/storage/, /content/databases/, etc).

I think this PR is ready to be re-reviewed now.  A couple items of note:

- I think I've reimplemented all of the old `OpticalPSF` using the `PhaseScreenPSF` framework now.  The new version can be accessed via `galsim.OpticalPSF`, and for now I've left the original accessible via `galsim.optics.OpticalPSF`.  The new version essentially passes the old unit tests (with a few minor tweaks).
- I reimplemented Table2D without wrapping InterpolatedImage.  It's ~5x faster this way.
- phase_psf.py is, unfortunately, too big to comment on directly in github.com now (see https://help.github.com/articles/what-are-the-limits-for-viewing-content-and-diffs-in-my-repository/#diff-limits).  I haven't figured out a good way to comment on individual lines with a "local Git client" so far.  Maybe we should temporarily split phase_psf.py up so it's easier to review?
- Integrating OpticalPSF in the new framework revealed I was previously missing some factors of 2 and pi when creating good pupil screen sizes and scales.  These are now fixed (as evidence by the OpticalPSF unit tests passing), but now the Jee+Tyson(2011) example in the `Atmosphere` docstring now takes ~3 hrs(!) to make a single PSF.  The fundamental problem is that the effective maxK for the Fourier optics is set by the diameter of the aperture, and actually completely ignores the wiggliness of the final integrated PSF.  So for roughly LSST-like params, even though a 15 second Atmospheric PSF may approach a Kolmogorov with maxK ~ 20 over time, the instantaneous PSFs all require maxK ~ 400, significantly slowing things down.  Probably some speed can safely be regained by setting `oversampling=1.0`, (and maybe even 0.5?), but I haven't changed this so far.
- Which brings me to my last point.  I think the code is in pretty good shape, but I'm not so sure about default values like `oversampling`, `pad_factor`, `time_step`, and probably more, especially for atmospheric PSFs.  These are next on my list to investigate, but I'm not sure if they belong in this PR or a subsequent one.

Thanks very much David for figuring this out.  You'll be heartened to hear that the next major GalSim release will probably be the one where I'm ripping out the boost dependency.  So hopefully that will save people a lot of headaches like this one.

Sure. I'm using something like this for an app I have which manages 3rd party projects

The distilled usage is something like. 
```
from gcloud import resource_manager
client = resource_manager.Client() // Client uses 1-st party service account credentials

def create_user_callback(user_id, user_email):
    """ Called when a user creates an account """
    project = client.new_project('managed-project-{}'.format(user_id))
    project.add_roll('roles/owner', 'user:{}'.format(user_email))

def delete_user_callback(user_id, user_email):
    """ Called when a user deletes their account """
    project = client.fetch_project('managed-project-{}'.format(user_id))
    project.remove_roll('roles/owner', 'user:{}'.format(user_email) // prevent user from undeleting
```

Obviously the same use case applies to pretty much any resource, for users that are wrapping our services on behalf of customers, or want to use our built in ACLs for any reason.

This represents more or less what I've already built. Obviously the method signatures are a little rough, and could be cleaned up by:

* wrapping "member" strings as types... so `resource.add_roll('roles/owner', 'user:{}'.format(user_email))` becomes `resource.add_roll('roles/owner', iam.User(user_email))`
* having implementing resources provide enums for the possible roles.
so `resource.add_roll('roles/owner', 'user:{}'.format(user_email))` becomes `resource.add_roll(resource.OWNER, 'user:{}'.format(user_email))`

Not sure how I feel about both of these, but open to input.

- Permissions/roles to make the request to the API endpoint.
- Authorization to charge that request to a user-supplied project.  Given the explicit `userProject` query paramter being passed, I'm assuming that the project-to-be-charged is not (at least necessarily) the one primarily associated with the callers credentials:  otherwise, the back-end could just charge the "primary" project without needing the `userProject` at all.

Adding some notes based on digging around...

Both views and tables are normally defined in `pg_table_def` (a catalog table) and this is what is currently used in [_get_all_column_info](https://github.com/sqlalchemy-redshift/sqlalchemy-redshift/blob/1c9baf1fcc3c6f2c7044880bdd094c436c341d29/sqlalchemy_redshift/dialect.py#L721).

External table information isn't in pg catalog tables. Views that include external tables aren't, either, because they are required to use [WITH NO SCHEMA BINDING](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/redshift/latest/dg/r_CREATE_VIEW.html#r_CREATE_VIEW-parameters). In fact, describing these views in the shell (with `/d`) doesn't even show anything useful.

Metadata about tables can be found in `svv_external_tables`:

```
# \d svv_external_tables       
  View "pg_catalog.svv_external_tables"  
      Column       |  Type   | Modifiers 
-------------------+---------+-----------
 schemaname        | text    |           
 tablename         | text    |           
 location          | text    |           
 input_format      | text    |           
 output_format     | text    |           
 serialization_lib | text    |           
 serde_parameters  | text    |           
 compressed        | integer |           
 parameters        | text    |           
```

Metadata for views that include tables can be found in [pg_get_late_binding_view_cols()](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/redshift/latest/dg/PG_GET_LATE_BINDING_VIEW_COLS.html). One example for querying this from the docs:

```sql
select * from pg_get_late_binding_view_cols() cols(view_schema name, view_name name, col_name name, col_type varchar, col_num int);
```

I'm still digging into this but what I need, ideally, is to be able to use `Introspector.get_columns` to return column meta data from an external table.
